event_handlers: onInit: runs on page load; reads URL parameters; calls getEventFilterOptions; resolves initial selected_year, selected_month, and selected_event_type; if necessary calls getEventDetails(selected_event_id) to derive year/month; writes resolved filters back into the URL; calls listEvents with the resolved filter values; if selected_event_id is present, ensures corresponding detail data is loaded and signals the event-detail-panel to open.

onURLParamChange: triggered when the browser back/forward navigation alters the query string; re-reads year, month, event_type, and event_id; validates them against month_options and event_types; re-calls listEvents with the resulting filters and getEventDetails if event_id is present; updates child components with the new state.

onFilterChange: called when the events-filter-bar reports that the selected year/month or event_type changed; updates internal selected_year, selected_month, and selected_event_type; updates year, month, and event_type in the URL (removing event_type when cleared); removes any event_id parameter; calls listEvents(selected_year, selected_month, selected_event_type) to refresh the list; instructs the event-detail-panel to close if it is open.

onSelectEvent: called when events-list reports that an event was clicked; sets selected_event_id to the clicked Event.id; updates event_id in the URL while preserving current year, month, and event_type; calls getEventDetails(selected_event_id) if the details are not already cached; passes the loaded event, location, and price_tiers to the event-detail-panel to display.

onCloseEventDetails: called when the event-detail-panel is closed; clears selected_event_id; removes event_id from the URL; leaves year, month, and event_type untouched; ensures no detail content is shown.

onDetailsLoadedForDeepLink: internal handler used when event_id is present but year/month are missing; receives event data from getEventDetails, derives year and month from event.start_datetime, selects a matching month_option if available, updates selected_year and selected_month, writes these into the URL, and then calls listEvents with the resolved filters.

components.functionality: Provides controls for selecting a month/year and event type filter for the events list. It displays month/year options and event type options obtained from getEventFilterOptions and reflects the currently active filter state derived from URL parameters.
data_binding: month_options (array from getEventFilterOptions, each with year, month, and label)
event_types (array from getEventFilterOptions, each with key and label)
event_handlers: onChangeMonthOption: fired when the user selects a different month/year option; extracts year and month from the chosen month_options entry; sends the new year and month back to events-page-controller via onFilterChange; does not directly call listEvents.

onChangeEventType: fired when the user selects or clears an event type; captures the chosen event_types.key (or null/undefined when cleared) and sends it back to events-page-controller via onFilterChange; does not directly call listEvents.

onSyncFromState: invoked when events-page-controller updates selected_year, selected_month, or selected_event_type (for example after a URL change); the filter bar updates its control values so the UI matches the current filter state.

components.functionality: Displays the list of events returned by listEvents for the currently selected filters. Each item is interactive and can be used to open the detailed view of that event.
data_binding: Event (array from listEvents; each item includes at least id, title, subtitle, description, event_type, venue_name, start_datetime, end_datetime, status, image_url, and seasonId when available)
Derived filter context (selected_year, selected_month, selected_event_type from events-page-controller) for showing contextual information such as the active filter summary.
event_handlers: onClickEventItem: triggered when the user clicks an event entry; emits the Event.id to events-page-controller via onSelectEvent; does not modify URL parameters directly.

onEmptyStateRender: invoked when the bound Event array from listEvents is empty; displays a non-data-dependent message indicating that no events match the current filters (for example, encouraging the user to try a different month or event type).
onSyncFromState: called when events-page-controller updates the bound Event array after calling listEvents; causes the list to re-render with the current results.

components.functionality: Shows detailed information for a selected event, including its description, date and time, venue/location information, and an overview of available ticket price tiers. Provides a call-to-action button to navigate into the ticket selection flow for the selected event.
data_binding: selected_event_id (from events-page-controller via URL parameter event_id)
Location (from getEventDetails.location, used to display address and venue context when available)
TicketPriceTier (array from getEventDetails.price_tiers, used to present an overview of ticket pricing tiers, sorted by sort_order when provided)
Season (optional; resolved by matching Event.seasonId to the Season array from getEventFilterOptions when the controller provides it)
event_handlers: onOpenWithEventId: invoked by events-page-controller when selected_event_id is set; receives the Event, Location, and TicketPriceTier data from getEventDetails; displays the detail panel in an open state.

onClickBuyTickets: triggered when the user clicks the primary call-to-action button; reads the currently bound selected_event_id and initiates navigation to tickets.html?event_id=<selected_event_id>; does not change any URL parameters on events.html itself.
onClickClose: triggered when the user closes the detail panel; emits a close event to events-page-controller, which in turn clears selected_event_id and removes the event_id parameter from the URL.

onStatusAwareRender: adjusts the availability or messaging around the 'Buy Tickets' or 'Select Seats' button based on Event.status and the presence or absence of active TicketPriceTier entries (for example, disabling the button when the event is cancelled or there are no active tiers).

components.functionality: Encapsulates logic for reading and writing URL query parameters related to events and filters. Ensures that year, month, event_type, and event_id in the URL accurately reflect the current UI state and that any changes to the URL (including browser navigation) are propagated back into the page controller.
data_binding: URL parameters: year (number), month (number), event_type (string), event_id (string)
Derived state mapping between URL parameters and controller state (selected_year, selected_month, selected_event_type, selected_event_id)
event_handlers: onReadFromURL: called on page load and in response to browser navigation events; parses the current query string into year, month, event_type, and event_id; passes these values to events-page-controller.onInit or onURLParamChange.

onUpdateURL: called by events-page-controller whenever selected_year, selected_month, selected_event_type, or selected_event_id change; writes the corresponding year, month, event_type, and event_id parameters into the query string (removing parameters whose values are cleared) using pushState or replaceState semantics.
onListenHistoryEvents: attaches to browser history events (such as popstate) and triggers onReadFromURL so the rest of the page updates in response to back/forward actions.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header. The page follows a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom reading order: (1) the page-controller area provides the page title and top-level framing; (2) the filter bar appears as a compact horizontal band just below the title; (3) the events list occupies the main body of the page; (4) the event detail panel expands in-line within the list when needed. The URL state manager is non-visual and does not affect physical layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header. The page follows a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom reading order: (1) the page-controller area provides the page title and top-level framing; (2) the filter bar appears as a compact horizontal band just below the title; (3) the events list occupies the main body of the page; (4) the event detail panel expands in-line within the list when needed. The URL state manager is non-visual and does not affect physical layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content column is constrained to a max width of roughly 960–1140px and centered within the viewport, with generous white margins on larger screens. Vertically, the page-controller/title band uses generous top and bottom margins (about 64px above, 48–64px below) to echo the mockup’s prominent title treatment. The filter bar takes a modest vertical slice with 16–24px padding. The events list then consumes the majority of the vertical space, with each card generously padded and separated. When visible, the event detail panel temporarily occupies a substantial vertical block directly in the main column, similar in visual weight to a feature article block.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a low content density throughout: large title, ample whitespace, and clear breathing room between the title, filters, each event card, and the detail panel. Information is grouped into distinct blocks with visible separation instead of dense, app-like clusters. Internal component padding generally follows 16–24px, with 24–32px vertical gaps between major blocks, preserving a calm, editorial rhythm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves top-down: logo/nav (outside these components), then the large left-aligned page title in the center column, then down to the understated filter bar, and onward into the vertical sequence of event cards. Within that flow, interaction pulls focus from a selected event card into an in-line detail panel directly beneath it, without breaking the single-column flow. No elements float or dock to the sides; everything aligns to the same central axis, emphasizing a steady, linear reading experience reminiscent of a concert program.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width site header, a single centered content column fills the middle of the screen, framed by generous white margins on the left and right. At the top of this column, the Events & Concerts page-controller presents a large, dignified serif title with ample white space above and below, visually forming its own band across the column. Directly beneath, a compact, lightly bordered filter bar spans a comfortable portion of the column width, its controls laid out in a single horizontal row but still aligned to the same left edge as the title. Below this bar, the events list continues the vertical flow: a series of full-width-in-column event cards stacked one after another, each separated by clear vertical gaps so the eye can rest between entries. When an event is selected, a detail panel appears in-line beneath that event’s card, expanding into a deeper, editorial-style block of information while staying centered in the same column. Throughout, the layout feels calm and ordered: one main column, generous breathing room, and a top-down narrative that mirrors a printed program or season brochure, rather than a dense application interface.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-page-controller occupies the entire main content column beneath the global site header. The column itself is centered in the viewport, with a max-width of approximately 960–1140px and equal white margins on the left and right. At the very top of this column, about 64px below the bottom edge of the header, the page-controller presents the page title 'Events & Concerts' in large serif type, left-aligned within the column but visually centered on the page due to the symmetric white margins. There is generous whitespace above and below the title: approximately 48–64px separating it from the header above and about 32–48px before the next component (the filter bar). If a short subtitle or descriptive sentence is included by this controller, it appears directly beneath the title, left-aligned, within the same column width and with a smaller gap (about 16–24px) below the title, still maintaining a spacious, airy feeling. The page-controller effectively acts as the outer container and vertical stack owner for the filter bar, events list, and any in-line detail panels, keeping all of them aligned to the same central axis and ensuring consistent margins and vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as part of the normal document flow; establishes the main centered content container and anchors all subsequent components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-filter-bar sits directly beneath the page title area within the same centered content column. It begins roughly 32–48px below the bottom of the title (or subtitle, if present), preserving the generous band of whitespace around the title while clearly marking the start of interactive browsing controls. Horizontally, the filter bar spans most of the content column—around 80–90% of the column width—remaining centered so that there is a small, even margin on both the left and right relative to the column edges. The bar itself reads as a light, understated horizontal panel (e.g., a subtle border or faint background), with internal padding of about 16–20px on all sides. Filter controls (month/year dropdown, event type selector, etc.) are arranged in a single left-to-right row, left-aligned within the bar, with consistent gaps of about 16px between controls. On larger screens, the right side of the bar remains mostly open white space, reinforcing a low-density feel. Vertically, there is a modest gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the filter bar and the first event card in the events list, so the bar feels visually connected to the content it controls but still distinct as its own layer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls away naturally with content; no sticky or floating behavior, maintaining a calm, editorial browsing experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-list occupies the main body of the page, directly below the filter bar, within the same centered column. It begins roughly 24–32px beneath the filter bar, with no visual frame of its own—the list appears as a natural continuation of the page’s vertical narrative. It spans the full width of the content column (up to the 960–1140px max), with its top edge aligned to the same left margin as the title and filters. Inside this area, event entries are presented as vertically stacked cards or rows, each card taking 100% of the column width. Each event card has internal padding of about 16–24px and is separated from the next card by a vertical gap of 24–32px, maintaining a clear, spacious rhythm. If events are grouped by month or season, the month headings appear as simple, left-aligned text labels spanning the column width, with slightly larger gaps (32–40px) above them to suggest a new section, echoing an editorial layout. As the user scrolls, the list continues downward in a linear fashion; the eye can easily scan from one event title to the next without visual clutter. When a detail panel is open for a particular event, it inserts directly into this list flow (see detail panel description), pushing subsequent event cards downward but preserving the single-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupies the main scrolling region; event entries stack vertically in a single column without side-by-side grids, supporting a clear top-to-bottom reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-panel is an in-line expansion that appears within the same centered content column and in the same visual band as the events list. By default (no event selected), the panel is not visible and occupies no space. When a user selects an event from the list, the detail panel materializes directly beneath that specific event’s card, as if a more detailed article about that event has unfolded inside the list. It spans the full width of the content column, aligned perfectly with the left and right edges of the event cards. The panel has generous internal padding—around 24–32px on all sides—and is visually distinguished by either a subtle border, a very light background tint, or slightly increased vertical spacing above and below (e.g., 16–24px more than between standard cards). The vertical height of the panel is allowed to grow as needed, effectively becoming a prominent block that might occupy a third to half of the visible viewport height or more, depending on the amount of content (description, date/time, venue, ticket button). A small vertical gap of around 16–24px separates the bottom of the triggering event card from the top of the detail panel, and a similar gap separates the bottom of the panel from the next event card below, so the whole expansion still reads as part of the same editorial column rather than as a separate overlay. Any call-to-action (e.g., 'Select Tickets') appears toward the bottom of this panel, left-aligned within the content, maintaining the formal and restrained style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within the normal document flow, expanding the vertical height of the page and pushing subsequent content downward; it does not float or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is non-visual and does not occupy any visible space on the page. Conceptually, it is attached to the overall page-controller and listens to changes in filters, current page of results, and any selected event for the detail panel. Its presence has no direct impact on layout: it neither alters spacing nor introduces any visible container. Instead, it synchronizes the state of the filter bar, events list, and event detail panel with the URL and browser history behind the scenes, preserving the editorial layout while enabling deep links and back/forward navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No spatial behavior; it exists purely as a background state manager with zero visual or spatial footprint in the layout.

name: Ticket Selection
title: Ticket Selection
description: Select a ticket price tier and specific seats for a single event, then add the selection to the ticket cart and continue to checkout.

core_features: Read the required event_id (and optional price_tier_id) URL parameters and load event ticketing data by calling getTicketSelectionData(eventId).
Display basic event context (title, date/time, venue) using the Event returned from getTicketSelectionData to confirm the user is selecting tickets for the correct event.

List all active TicketPriceTier options for the event, with clear labeling of which tiers are considered mid-priced according to a configurable price threshold (no hardcoded amount) or derived mid-range tiers.

Preselect a price tier based on the price_tier_id URL parameter when it matches an active tier; otherwise, select a sensible default tier (e.g., a mid-priced tier if available, falling back to the lowest-priced active tier).
Filter the Seat collection from getTicketSelectionData by the selected price tier and show a seating chart view that exposes seat availability and allows selection of seats only within that tier.

Enable users to select seats, with logic to help them select adjacent seats (especially when the desired quantity is two), and prevent selection of seats that are not available (e.g., Seat.status not equal to 'available').
Maintain a ticket quantity that always matches the number of selected seats, allowing the user to set or confirm the quantity through seat selection and a quantity control that stays synchronized.
Show a running selection summary including selected tier, number of tickets, selected seat labels, and calculated subtotal using the TicketPriceTier.price value from getTicketSelectionData.

Provide an Add to Cart / Continue action that calls selectSeatsAndAddToCart(eventId, priceTierId, seatIds) with the currently selected tier and seats, then redirects to checkout.html?from=tickets on success.
Handle error and concurrency states from selectSeatsAndAddToCart (such as seats no longer available) by showing a clear error message and refreshing seat availability as needed.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive from event details with a suggested tier
1) User clicks a ticket purchase link on events.html.
2) Browser navigates to tickets.html?event_id={Event.id}&price_tier_id={TicketPriceTier.id}.
3) On page load, the Ticket Selection page reads event_id and price_tier_id from the URL.
4) The page calls getTicketSelectionData(eventId=event_id).
5) When the data is returned, the page displays the Event details and all active TicketPriceTier options.
6) The price_tier_id from the URL is matched against the returned price_tiers; if found and active, that tier is preselected.
7) Seats are filtered to only those with Seat.priceTierId equal to the selected tier, and the seating chart is rendered.
8) User selects seats (e.g., two adjacent seats), which updates the current selection and ticket quantity.
9) User reviews the selection summary and clicks Add to Cart / Continue.
10) The page calls selectSeatsAndAddToCart(eventId=event_id, priceTierId=selectedTierId, seatIds=[selectedSeatIds]).
11) If success is true, the user is redirected to checkout.html?from=tickets.
12) If success is false, the page displays message and refreshes seat availability by re-calling getTicketSelectionData(eventId).

Workflow 2: Arrive directly with only event_id
1) User opens tickets.html?event_id={Event.id} directly (e.g., from a saved link or index.html).
2) On page load, the Ticket Selection page reads event_id, detects that price_tier_id is absent, and validates that event_id is present.
3) The page calls getTicketSelectionData(eventId=event_id).
4) The Event and price_tiers are rendered; the page computes which tiers qualify as mid-priced based on configuration or derived mid-range and highlights them.
5) The default selected tier is chosen (e.g., preferred mid-priced tier or lowest-priced active tier) and reflected both in the UI and by updating the URL to include price_tier_id for this selection (without reloading the page).
6) The seating chart for the selected tier is drawn using Seat records filtered by eventId and priceTierId.
7) User adjusts quantity and selects the desired number of seats; seat selection logic ensures the quantity control always matches the count of selected seats.
8) User clicks Add to Cart / Continue, triggering selectSeatsAndAddToCart.
9) On success, user is redirected to checkout.html?from=tickets; on failure, an error message is shown and the user can adjust their selection.

Workflow 3: Change price tier mid-process
1) User is on tickets.html with an event loaded and a price tier selected.
2) User clicks a different TicketPriceTier option in the list.
3) The selected tier state is updated, and the URL is updated to reflect price_tier_id={newTierId}.
4) Any previously selected seats are cleared, and the ticket quantity resets to match the cleared selection.
5) The seating chart re-renders using Seat records filtered by the new priceTierId.
6) User selects seats within the new tier; quantity and summary update accordingly.
7) User can then proceed with Add to Cart / Continue, which calls selectSeatsAndAddToCart using the new priceTierId and associated seatIds.

Workflow 4: Handle seat availability conflicts when adding to cart
1) User has selected seats and clicks Add to Cart / Continue.
2) The page calls selectSeatsAndAddToCart(eventId, priceTierId, seatIds).
3) The interface response returns success=false, along with a message and possibly a list of held_seat_ids that are no longer available.
4) The page displays the message from the response, visually marks any seats that became unavailable, and removes them from the user’s selection and quantity.
5) The page may optionally re-call getTicketSelectionData(eventId) to fully refresh seat statuses.
6) User re-selects seats and retries Add to Cart / Continue until success=true and the redirect to checkout.html?from=tickets occurs.
interactions: On initial load, read event_id (required) and price_tier_id (optional) from the query string; if event_id is missing, show an error state and do not call getTicketSelectionData.
On initial load (when event_id is present), call getTicketSelectionData(eventId=event_id) once to retrieve Event, price_tiers, and seats data.

On successful retrieval of ticket selection data, sort and filter price_tiers to only those with is_active=true, then compute which tiers are mid-priced based on a configurable threshold or derived mid-range logic and flag them for display.

When a price tier is clicked in the tier list, update the selected priceTierId state, clear any currently selected seats, recalculate mid-priced highlighting if needed, and update the page URL to set price_tier_id to the new selection without reloading.

When rendering the seating chart, filter seats to those where Seat.eventId equals the current event_id and Seat.priceTierId equals the selected priceTierId, excluding seats where Seat.status is not 'available'.

When a user clicks on a seat in the seating chart, toggle that seat’s selection state if it is available; if adding a seat would exceed the intended quantity or break adjacency rules (for specific flows such as selecting two adjacent seats), show a message or prevent the selection according to defined logic.

Whenever the set of selected seats changes, update the ticket quantity to exactly match the number of selected seats and recalculate the subtotal using selectedSeats.length multiplied by the selected TicketPriceTier.price.

If the user adjusts ticket quantity directly (e.g., via a quantity input or stepper), adjust the expected number of seats correspondingly, guiding the user to select or deselect seats so that the number of selected seats equals the quantity; Add to Cart is disabled until they match.

On clicking Add to Cart / Continue, validate that there is a selected price tier and at least one selected seat, that all selected seats are still considered available in the current client-side state, and only then call selectSeatsAndAddToCart(eventId, priceTierId, seatIds).

After selectSeatsAndAddToCart returns, if success is true, navigate the browser to checkout.html?from=tickets; if success is false, display the provided message and, if held_seat_ids are included, mark those seats as unavailable and remove them from current selection.

If the user navigates back or refreshes the page, use the current URL parameters (event_id and price_tier_id) to reconstruct the event context and preselected price tier, so the user returns to a consistent state.

If getTicketSelectionData or selectSeatsAndAddToCart encounters a network or system error, show an error banner or inline message and allow the user to retry the action without losing selected URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary source of state for this page is the URL query parameters and the data returned from getTicketSelectionData. The required event_id parameter identifies which Event and associated TicketPriceTier and Seat records to load. On page initialization, if event_id is missing, the page shows an error and does not attempt any API calls. When event_id is present, getTicketSelectionData(eventId=event_id) is called to obtain the Event (context), price_tiers, and seats. The optional price_tier_id parameter is used to attempt to preselect a specific TicketPriceTier. If price_tier_id is present and matches an is_active TicketPriceTier for the event, that tier becomes the selected tier. If price_tier_id is missing or invalid, the page determines a default tier (for example a mid-priced tier if identifiable, otherwise the lowest-priced active tier) and then updates the URL to include price_tier_id for this selected tier using history APIs to avoid a full reload. Whenever the user changes the selected tier, the in-memory selectedTierId state is updated and the URL's price_tier_id parameter is updated to keep the state persistent on reload or navigation. Seat selection state (the list of selected seatIds) lives in memory for the current page session and is always constrained by the current selectedTierId and the Seat records loaded from getTicketSelectionData. The ticket quantity state is derived from and synchronized with the count of selected seatIds, so it does not need its own external persistence; if the user reloads the page, seat selections and quantity reset, but event_id and price_tier_id restore the event and tier context. Calls to selectSeatsAndAddToCart do not alter URL parameters; instead, if successful, they immediately cause navigation to checkout.html with a from=tickets parameter to indicate the source page to the checkout flow.

components.functionality: Reads event_id and optional price_tier_id from the URL query string, validates the parameters, and calls getTicketSelectionData(eventId) to retrieve Event, TicketPriceTier, and Seat data. It initializes page-level state for the current event, available tiers, and seat availability. If event_id is missing or the API call fails, it exposes an error state for other components to render.

Displays concise contextual information for the current Event (such as title, date/time, and venue) so users can confirm they are selecting tickets for the correct concert or event. It updates automatically when the Event object from getTicketSelectionData changes.

Renders a list of all active TicketPriceTier options for the current Event, including tier name, description, and price. It computes and visually flags mid-priced tiers based on either a configurable price threshold or derived mid-range logic, without hardcoding a specific currency amount. It manages the selection of a single active tier, updates the selected tier state, and triggers URL updates for price_tier_id.

Displays seat availability for the current Event and selected TicketPriceTier as an interactive seating map or list. It filters Seat records to those matching the current event_id and selected priceTierId and with status indicating availability. Users can click seats to select or deselect them. The component enforces that only seats in the currently selected tier can be chosen and can provide additional guidance or constraints to help users select adjacent seats (for example by suggesting or automatically pairing neighboring seats when a quantity of two is desired). It maintains and exposes the list of selected seatIds.

Shows and manages the ticket quantity associated with the current selection. The primary source of truth is the count of selected seats from the seating-chart component. If users adjust the quantity directly (for example via plus/minus buttons), the component updates the expected quantity and prompts the user to add or remove seat selections so that the number of selected seats matches. It disables the Add to Cart action until quantity equals the number of selected seats.

Summarizes the current ticket choice, including selected Event context, chosen TicketPriceTier, number of tickets (derived from selected seats), list or count of selected seat labels (e.g., using Seat.row and Seat.seat_number), and the computed subtotal using TicketPriceTier.price multiplied by the number of selected seats. It updates in real time as the user changes the tier or seat selections and surfaces validation messages if the selection is incomplete (such as no tier selected or zero seats selected).

Provides the primary call-to-action button (e.g., Add to Cart or Continue). Before enabling, it checks that an Event is loaded, a TicketPriceTier is selected, and at least one seat is selected, with ticket quantity matching the number of selected seats. When clicked, it calls selectSeatsAndAddToCart(eventId, priceTierId, seatIds) using the current state. Based on the response, it either redirects to checkout.html?from=tickets (when success is true) or displays an error message and optionally updates seat availability when success is false.

Displays validation errors, API error messages, and informational notices related to ticket selection and cart operations. It listens for errors from getTicketSelectionData and selectSeatsAndAddToCart, as well as client-side validation failures such as missing event_id, no tier selected, or mismatched quantity and seats. When the selectSeatsAndAddToCart response includes held_seat_ids, this component triggers visual feedback in the seating-chart to mark affected seats as unavailable and prompts the user to adjust their selection.

Keeps the URL query parameters in sync with the current page state and interprets them on load. Specifically, it ensures that event_id remains set to the current Event.id and that price_tier_id reflects the currently selected TicketPriceTier.id. When the user changes the selected price tier, it updates the price_tier_id parameter using the browser history API to avoid reloads. It also attaches the from=tickets parameter when programmatic navigation to checkout.html is initiated after a successful cart update.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: All ticketing components sit within a single centered main content column (roughly 960–1140px max-width) beneath the site header. The components are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order: event context/title region, system messages, ticket price and quantity controls, seating chart, selection summary, and finally the add-to-cart action. Invisible system components (loaders, URL sync) are conceptually attached to this column but do not occupy visible space. This preserves the mockup’s simple, columnar, editorial layout with no heavy sidebars or app-like panels.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All ticketing components sit within a single centered main content column (roughly 960–1140px max-width) beneath the site header. The components are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order: event context/title region, system messages, ticket price and quantity controls, seating chart, selection summary, and finally the add-to-cart action. Invisible system components (loaders, URL sync) are conceptually attached to this column but do not occupy visible space. This preserves the mockup’s simple, columnar, editorial layout with no heavy sidebars or app-like panels.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top of the page grants generous vertical space to the event summary area, echoing the large title band from the mockup. The seating chart becomes the main visual block, analogous to the hero image, spanning most of the content width and consuming the largest vertical area. The price tier list and selection summary are shorter textual blocks with comfortable breathing room above and below (about 48–64px between major sections, 24–32px between related elements). The add-to-cart button sits in its own small band of whitespace, visually separated but clearly tied to the summary above.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays low throughout: each component is given generous margins and internal padding (16–24px) with clear separation between sections. The page avoids cramped multi-column forms; instead, options are presented in calm, readable blocks. Text-heavy elements like the price tier list and summary use line spacing and spacing between rows consistent with an editorial layout, maintaining open white space around the dominant seating chart image block.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves top-down: from the header (logo/nav) to the event summary (serving as the page’s main typographic focal point), then down to any status messages, then naturally into the price tiers and quantity control. From there, the flow drops to the large, central seating chart, which functions as the visual hero. Below the chart, attention shifts to the ticket selection summary (confirming choices) and finally rests on the centered add-to-cart button. Horizontal scanning is minimal: primarily within the price tier rows (label, description, price) and within the seating chart’s own internal labeling, keeping the overall experience vertical and composed.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width site header, the page content sits in a centered, white-backed column that feels like a refined concert program page. At the top of this column, a spacious event summary area presents the event name and essentials with prominent serif typography, surrounded by generous top and bottom whitespace. Directly underneath, any ticketing errors or status messages appear as a slim band, spanning the column but not overwhelming it. The next band presents the available ticket price tiers in a vertically stacked list, with each tier occupying a full row and aligned neatly like lines in a program. Just below, a modest quantity control sits aligned with the tiers, keeping the selection context together without crowding. Dropping further down, the layout opens into a large, central seating chart block that visually echoes the hero image from the mockup, framed by white margins on both sides and occupying the largest visual footprint. Beneath this, a lightly framed summary panel gathers the user’s current selections and totals in a compact, readable section. Finally, the add-to-cart button sits centered below the summary, with ample space around it, serving as the clear call-to-action without feeling loud or app-like. Invisible loader and URL-sync components support this flow behind the scenes, leaving the visible presentation clean, calm, and dignified.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates invisibly and does not render a visible element in the layout. Conceptually, it is anchored to the top of the main content column, initiating data loading for the event before or as the visible event summary appears. If a loading indicator is needed, it would present as a subtle, centered text line or spinner directly within the top of the content column, in the space where the event summary will appear, maintaining the same centered alignment and ample surrounding whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background logic associated with the main content column; any optional loading indicator would be inline and centered, not fixed or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the top of the main content column, immediately below the site header, this block functions like the large title band in the mockup. It spans the full width of the central column (approximately 80–90% of the browser width on large screens, capped at ~960–1140px), left-aligned text within that column but visually centered on the page. The event title uses large serif typography and occupies the top of this block, followed by smaller lines for date, time, venue, and possibly a brief subtitle. There is generous vertical spacing: roughly 48–64px of whitespace above it (from the header) and 32–48px below it before the next section, giving the event summary clear prominence as the first focal element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main document flow; does not stick or float on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly below the event summary (with about 32–48px of vertical separation), the price-tier list occupies a single band across the main content column. It is centered with the column and spans roughly 70–80% of the column width, leaving generous left and right margins to keep the page feeling airy. Each price tier appears as a full-width row: tier name and description left-aligned, price right-aligned on the same baseline, mimicking a program line item. Rows have 16–24px vertical padding and 8–16px spacing between them, maintaining a low-density, easy-to-scan list. Selection indicators (radio buttons or similar) sit aligned to the left of each row, but the rows remain clean and largely text-driven, echoing the editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked list; it expands downward as more tiers are present but remains within the central column without scrolling independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The seating chart sits below the price-tier and quantity region, separated by about 32–48px of whitespace so it reads as the next major section. It is the largest visual element on the page, analogous to the hero image in the mockup. The chart is centered within the main content column and spans approximately 80–90% of that column width, framed by white margins on both sides. Its height is substantial—roughly equal to or greater than the height of the event summary and price tier sections combined—so it occupies the central portion of the viewport on desktop. The chart area is contained within a subtle, light border or faint shadow to differentiate it from the white background while maintaining a refined aesthetic, with 16–24px internal padding around any labels or legends. Above the chart, a small, centered heading (e.g., “Select Your Seats”) can sit with 16–24px spacing, reinforcing its role as the primary interactive canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable only as part of the page; the chart itself may support internal panning/zooming but its container remains fixed within the linear vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket quantity control is placed just below the price-tier list, with a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px so it feels clearly associated but not cramped. It aligns with the left edge of the price-tier text within the main column, occupying only a portion of the width (roughly 40–50% of the column), leaving open space to the right to preserve the airy layout. The control elements (label, minus/plus buttons or dropdown, and quantity value) are arranged horizontally but kept compact, with 8–16px internal padding. The overall block height is modest, visually lighter than the price-tier rows and significantly smaller than the seating chart, reinforcing its supportive role. There is about 24–32px of whitespace below it before the seating chart begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block within the vertical flow; does not float or stick, and resizes or wraps naturally on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Placed directly below the seating chart with roughly 32–48px of vertical space, the ticket selection summary appears as a refined, card-like block within the main column. It spans around 60–70% of the column width and is centered horizontally, ensuring ample white space on both sides. The block has 16–24px of internal padding and may use a subtle border or tinted background to distinguish it from the page, mirroring a nicely framed section in a printed program. Inside, information (selected tier, seat numbers, quantities, subtotal) is laid out in a simple vertical stack or two-column typographic layout, left-aligned with clear line spacing. The overall height is moderate—taller than a single button but shorter than the seating chart—making it a clear but calm confirmation area before checkout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that scrolls with the page; no sticky behavior, maintaining the straightforward vertical reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart button sits below the ticket selection summary, separated by about 24–32px of whitespace so it is visually connected yet distinct as the final step. The button is centered within the main content column and does not stretch full-width; instead it occupies roughly 25–35% of the column width on desktop, preserving the understated, non-app-like feel. It has comfortable padding (about 8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal) and stands out primarily through contrast in color and typography rather than size. There is generous whitespace—approximately 48–64px—below the button before the page footer or any additional content, allowing it to feel like a considered, singular action rather than one of many competing controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static call-to-action button, not fixed; it remains in the normal scroll flow and is reached naturally after reading the summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is positioned between the event summary and the price-tier list, occupying a slim horizontal band that spans the full width of the main content column. When present, it appears about 16–24px below the bottom of the event summary, pushing the price tiers further down. Messages are left-aligned within this band, with 12–16px internal padding and perhaps a subtle colored accent line or muted background, keeping them noticeable but not visually aggressive. Multiple messages, if needed, stack vertically with small gaps (8–12px), but the block height remains modest so as not to overwhelm the calm layout. When no message is present, the space collapses entirely, preserving the clean vertical spacing between the summary and the price tiers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, appearing and disappearing within the document flow; no fixed positioning or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render any visible UI and therefore does not claim explicit spatial real estate. Conceptually, it is associated with the entire ticket selection area, updating the URL as the user changes tiers, seats, or quantities. If it ever needs to surface error text, that text would be channeled through the existing ticket-error-and-status-messages band to preserve the consistent layout and editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background behavior tied to the page state; no independent layout or positioning.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Page to review selected tickets and enter buyer contact information before proceeding to the final order review.

core_features: Fetch and display a checkout summary using getCheckoutSummary, including event details, selected cart items, seat information, price tiers, and computed amounts (subtotal, fees, total).
Show an itemized list of tickets (per CartItem) including event title, date/time, price tier, seat labels (derived from Seat and TicketPriceTier data), quantities, and line totals.
Display overall cost summary using subtotal_amount, fees_amount, and total_amount from getCheckoutSummary.
Provide a buyer information form to collect buyerFirstName, buyerLastName, and buyerEmail required for the order.
Validate buyer information client-side (required fields, basic email format) before calling updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder.
Call updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder on form submission to create or update a TicketOrder in "in_review" status.
After a successful updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder call, navigate to order_review.html with order_id set to the returned TicketOrder.id.
Support re-entry to the page using URL parameters (from and order_id) to adjust page messaging and workflow context (e.g., editing buyer info vs. initial entry).
user_workflows:
Initial checkout from ticket selection:
1. User completes seat and ticket selection on tickets.html and clicks a "Continue" action that submits to checkout.html, optionally including ?from=tickets.
2. checkout.html loads and invokes getCheckoutSummary with no parameters.
3. The page displays event information and an itemized list of all CartItems, with seat labels built from Seat data and price tiers from TicketPriceTier.
4. The user reviews the list to confirm quantities, seats, and pricing, and checks the subtotal, fees, and total.
5. The user enters buyer first name, last name, and email into the buyer information form.
6. The user clicks the "Review Order" button, triggering client-side validation.
7. If validation passes, the page calls updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder(buyerFirstName, buyerLastName, buyerEmail).
8. If the API returns success=true, the page reads order.id from the response and redirects to order_review.html?order_id={order.id}.
9. If the API returns success=false or an error, the page displays the returned message and does not navigate away.

Returning from order review to edit buyer information:
1. User is on order_review.html viewing an in-review TicketOrder and clicks an "Edit buyer information" action that links to checkout.html?order_id={TicketOrder.id}.
2. checkout.html initializes and reads order_id from the URL.
3. The page still calls getCheckoutSummary with no parameters to load the current cart and pricing context (relying on backend session/cart association rather than the URL parameter).
4. The presence of order_id causes the page state manager to adjust UI copy (for example, indicating that the user is updating existing buyer information) but does not change which APIs are called.
5. The user modifies buyerFirstName, buyerLastName, and/or buyerEmail in the buyer information form.
6. The user clicks the "Review Order" button.
7. The page validates the fields and calls updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder with the updated values. The backend associates this call to the existing in-review TicketOrder via its own context.
8. On success, the page redirects again to order_review.html?order_id={order.id} using the id from the response, so the user sees the updated buyer information reflected.

Simple review-only interaction (no changes made):
1. User lands on checkout.html (with or without URL parameters).
2. User reviews the ticket list and pricing without editing any fields.
3. User can navigate away using browser controls or optional navigation links (e.g., back to tickets.html) without calling updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder if they do not click the "Review Order" button.
interactions: On page load, the page reads URL parameters from and order_id to determine contextual state (e.g., whether the user is coming from tickets.html or editing an existing order).

On initial render, the page invokes getCheckoutSummary to retrieve TicketCart, Event, CartItem, Seat, TicketPriceTier, and aggregated amount fields, then binds the returned data to the ticket summary and totals components.
Users can scroll through and visually review each CartItem, including seat and price tier details derived dynamically from the API responses.
Users can interact with a buyer information form, entering and editing buyerFirstName, buyerLastName, and buyerEmail.

On buyer information form submission (clicking the "Review Order" button), client-side validation runs: all three fields must be non-empty; buyerEmail is checked against a simple email pattern; if validation fails, inline errors are displayed and no API call is made.
If validation passes, the page calls updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder with the current form field values.
If updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder returns success=true, the page reads order.id and navigates to order_review.html?order_id={order.id}.

If updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder returns success=false or an error occurs, the page displays the provided message near the form and keeps the user on checkout.html so they can correct input or try again.

If the user uses the browser's Back button from order_review.html to return to checkout.html without a full reload, the existing in-memory form state remains, allowing them to adjust and resubmit without re-typing.

Optional navigation interactions may include a link back to tickets.html that preserves context using URL parameters (for example, passing along an event identifier from the Event returned by getCheckoutSummary) so users can modify seat selection if needed.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses only in-memory state and URL parameters (no localStorage) to manage behavior. On initial load, the page parses the query string for two optional parameters: from and order_id. The from parameter, when present with a value such as 'tickets', is used purely to adjust contextual messaging and optional navigation (e.g., showing a 'Back to ticket selection' link). It does not change which APIs are called or their parameters. The order_id parameter, when present, indicates that the user may be editing buyer information for an existing in-review TicketOrder. The page still calls getCheckoutSummary without parameters; backend logic is responsible for mapping the current session or cart to the correct cart/order context. The presence of order_id affects UI text (e.g., indicating an update rather than a fresh submission) and can be used to ensure that, after a successful updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder call, the user is redirected to order_review.html?order_id={order.id}. All ticket and pricing data displayed on the page is sourced dynamically from getCheckoutSummary; no hardcoded ticket, event, seat, or pricing values are used. Buyer information is held only in memory while the user is on the page; it is sent to the backend exclusively via updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder. No state is persisted via localStorage or similar; persistence across pages is achieved only via backend-managed cart/order state and navigation using URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Checkout page by reading URL parameters, orchestrating the getCheckoutSummary call, controlling loading and error states, and providing contextual flags (e.g., isEditingExistingOrder) to other components.

Invokes getCheckoutSummary when the page is ready, stores the returned cart, event, items, seats, price tiers, and amount fields in page-level state for use by the ticket summary and totals components.

Displays an itemized list of CartItems for the current TicketCart, including event title and date/time, price tier names, seat labels built from Seat and TicketPriceTier data, quantities, unit prices, and line totals, using only data returned from getCheckoutSummary.

Shows subtotal, any fees, and total amount for the current cart, using the subtotal_amount, fees_amount, and total_amount fields returned by getCheckoutSummary. Reacts to loading or error states to avoid displaying incomplete or stale data.

Provides simple contextual text and optional navigation based on URL parameters and state. For example, indicates whether the user is starting checkout from ticket selection (from parameter present) or editing buyer info for an in-review order (order_id parameter present). May display a link back to tickets.html using event and from data when available.

Collects buyerFirstName, buyerLastName, and buyerEmail required to create or update a TicketOrder. Performs client-side validation, then uses updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder to send the data. Handles success by triggering navigation to order_review.html with the returned order_id and handles errors by displaying messages without leaving the page.

Listens for successful responses from updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder, extracts order.id from the returned TicketOrder, and performs a client-side redirect to order_review.html?order_id={order.id}. Ensures that navigation only occurs when success is true.

Displays errors related to loading the checkout summary (getCheckoutSummary failures) and to submitting buyer information (updateTicketBuyerInfoAndCreateOrder failures or validation errors). Ensures that error messages coming from the API response message field are shown without introducing hardcoded copies.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: All checkout components sit within a single, centered content column beneath the site header and the large 'Checkout' page title. Components are stacked in a top-to-bottom sequence that mirrors the mockup’s linear flow: contextual information first, then ticket review and totals, then buyer information, and finally the navigation control to proceed. There are no side-by-side app-like panels; instead, each block occupies its own horizontal band within the main column, with subtle internal alignments (e.g., totals right-aligned) to create rhythm while preserving the simple columnar structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All checkout components sit within a single, centered content column beneath the site header and the large 'Checkout' page title. Components are stacked in a top-to-bottom sequence that mirrors the mockup’s linear flow: contextual information first, then ticket review and totals, then buyer information, and finally the navigation control to proceed. There are no side-by-side app-like panels; instead, each block occupies its own horizontal band within the main column, with subtle internal alignments (e.g., totals right-aligned) to create rhythm while preserving the simple columnar structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered on the page and constrained to roughly 960–1140px max-width, with generous white margins on both left and right. The vertical spacing follows the mockup’s hierarchy: a substantial band of whitespace around the page title (outside this spec), then a moderate gap (48–64px) down to the first contextual element. Each major component (context banner, ticket summary, totals, form, navigation) is separated by 32–64px of vertical space. Within components, padding is generally 16–24px. The ticket summary and totals together occupy the upper half of the main content area, while the buyer form and navigation occupy the lower half, all framed by ample white space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low and editorial. Each component is allowed room to breathe with clear gaps between headings, lists, form fields, and buttons. There are no dense tables or compressed UI controls; instead, ticket rows are spaced, form fields are stacked with comfortable separation, and any supporting text is minimal and readable. Background remains predominantly white with occasional very light tints for emphasis, preserving a calm, dignified nonprofit aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves vertically down the centered column. After the fixed site header and prominent page title, the visitor sees a subtle context banner anchoring them in the checkout flow. Immediately below, the ticket summary list becomes the main focal area (analogous to the hero image in the mockup), followed by the more compact totals panel that naturally draws attention to the right end of the column. From there, the viewer’s gaze drops to the buyer information form and finally to the primary navigation control to proceed. Error messages, when present, appear high in the column (just below the context banner) so they are encountered early in the flow without disrupting the overall calm layout.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width header and a large, centered 'Checkout' title, the main content is presented as a single, centered column on a white background, constrained to a comfortable editorial width. At the top of this column, a low-profile context banner orients the user to the current step in the process. Directly beneath it, any error messages appear as a discreet but full-width bar, followed by a generous gap. The main focal block is the ticket summary: a clean, left-aligned list of selected tickets spanning the full content width within the column, much like a feature story or hero section in a program booklet. Immediately beneath the list, the totals panel is aligned toward the right edge of the same column, creating a subtle horizontal accent while staying within the vertical stack. Further down, the buyer information form occupies the majority of the column width, with well-spaced fields and labels that read like a carefully typeset form. At the bottom of the column, a centered navigation area with the primary “Continue to Review” action sits within a large field of whitespace, echoing the mockup’s generous margins and giving the page a calm, formal rhythm from top to bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page state manager is an invisible structural layer that conceptually wraps the entire main content column, from just below the page title down to just above the site footer. It does not render visible UI elements of its own but controls which of the other checkout components are shown at any given time. Spatially, it occupies the same centered width as the content column (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at 960–1140px) and stretches vertically to encompass all child components in their stacked top-to-bottom flow. When the page is in a loading or error state, this manager toggles between showing the loader, the error display, or the normal ticket/form content within that same column space, without changing the overall page framing or the generous white margins on either side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual wrapper; participates in normal document flow only as a container that determines when child components are displayed or hidden. It does not introduce extra padding or margins beyond what child components define.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary loader appears in place of the ticket summary and totals, occupying the same central band of the page. It is positioned in the main content column just below the context banner (and any error display), starting roughly 64px below the page title band. It spans the full width of the content column (about 70–80% of the viewport width, centered) with internal padding of around 24px on all sides. The loader’s visual elements—such as text placeholders or skeleton rows—are vertically stacked in a compact block that is about 1/3 of the viewport height on desktop, leaving generous white space above and below. This maintains the airy editorial feel while clearly signaling that ticket information is still loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal document flow, replacing the ticket summary list and totals panel while data is loading. Once loading is complete, it collapses and frees the vertical space for the actual content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket summary list is the first major content block in the checkout column and acts as the visual counterpart to the hero image in the mockup. It sits directly below the context banner and any error display, separated from them by a vertical gap of approximately 48px. The list spans the full width of the centered content column (up to about 960–1040px) and is left-aligned within that space. The block has comfortable internal padding (16–24px) but no heavy borders or shadows—at most a subtle bottom rule or thin top border—to preserve the understated, editorial look. Each ticket entry is arranged in a single row, with the ticket title and details aligned to the left and quantities/prices aligned to the right within the same column width, and vertical spacing of about 16–24px between rows. The overall height of this block typically occupies roughly 1/3 to 1/2 of the visible content area above the fold, framed by white space above (from the title and context) and below (before the totals panel).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow as a primary block in the central column. It expands vertically as more ticket rows are added but always remains within the same fixed-width centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The totals panel sits immediately beneath the ticket summary list, separated by a modest vertical gap of around 24–32px, forming a tight visual pair with the summary. It is positioned within the same centered content column but constrained to approximately 40–50% of that column’s width and aligned to the right edge, creating a subtle horizontal emphasis that draws the eye to the final amount without breaking the single-column structure. Internally, the panel uses 16–24px padding and vertically stacked lines for subtotal, fees/donations, and final total, with the grand total given a bit more whitespace above and a slightly larger font. The panel’s height is relatively modest—around 10–15% of the main content area—so that it reads as a compact calculation block anchored beneath the more expansive ticket list. White space beneath the panel (32–48px) clearly separates it from the buyer information form that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders in normal flow directly after the ticket summary list. On very narrow viewports, it can expand to full column width but retains its position directly beneath the list, keeping the vertical flow intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The context banner is the first visible element within the main content column and sits just below the large page title band, with a generous top margin of approximately 48–64px from the bottom of the title. It spans the full width of the centered column and has a low-profile height, roughly 10% of the main content height at most. The banner appears as a lightly tinted horizontal strip or a simple text block on white, with 16–20px of vertical padding and 24px of horizontal padding, housing a short line or two of text (e.g., step indicator, brief description) left-aligned for readability. The space below the banner—about 24–32px—is reserved for the error display when present, or flows directly into the ticket summary list when no errors exist. This placement ensures the banner gently frames the start of the checkout content without crowding the main ticket summary area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position in the vertical order at the top of the main content column; appears in normal flow and maintains consistent spacing relative to the page title above and content blocks below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The buyer information form is placed below the financial review sections, starting approximately 48–64px under the checkout totals panel. It spans most of the centered content column—around 70–90% of its width—so that form fields feel generous but still framed by side margins. The form is centered within the column and reads like a well-typeset editorial form: labels are left-aligned above each input, and fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px of vertical space between them and 24–32px between logical groups (e.g., contact info, billing address). The visual height of the form is substantial, potentially occupying 1/3 to 1/2 of the page on desktop, but the ample spacing and absence of heavy borders prevent it from feeling dense. A subtle section heading (e.g., 'Buyer Information') sits at the top of this block with a bit of extra spacing (24px) below, clearly differentiating it from the totals above. A generous margin (48–64px) is left below the last field before the navigation component, emphasizing the end of the form section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally as a large block in the centered column. It expands vertically as fields are added but retains the same left-aligned, single-column structure across desktop and smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review order navigation component is the final major element in the main content column and acts as the visual anchor at the bottom of the page. It sits directly below the buyer information form, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px so that the primary action feels clearly distinct from the last input field. Within the centered column, the primary button (e.g., 'Continue to Review Order') is horizontally centered and occupies a moderate width—roughly 25–40% of the column width—so it feels substantial but not overwhelming. Any secondary action (like 'Back' or 'Cancel') is aligned relative to this button (either to its left or just beneath it) with a smaller visual weight. The vertical padding around this component is generous, with an additional 64–80px of whitespace below before the footer area, echoing the mockup’s spacious treatment of key focal areas and giving the bottom of the page a calm, resolved feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal flow at the bottom of the main content column. It is not sticky or fixed; the user scrolls down to reach it, preserving the traditional, non-app-like experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error display is reserved a consistent position high in the main content column so that messages are encountered quickly without disrupting the overall composition. It sits directly below the context banner and above the ticket summary list, with approximately 16–24px separating it from the banner and another 24–32px separating it from the ticket summary. When present, it spans the full width of the centered column and has modest vertical padding (12–16px) with left-aligned text. The visual treatment is restrained—a thin border or slightly tinted background—so it stands out enough to be noticed but does not overpower the calm, editorial aesthetic. When no errors are present, this component collapses entirely, and the ticket summary list shifts up, occupying the space beneath the context banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in normal document flow, expanding vertically when errors exist and collapsing to zero height when there are none, while maintaining the same horizontal placement within the main content column.

name: Order Review
title: Order Review
description: Displays a read-only summary of a ticket order for a single event, allows returning to checkout to edit, and lets the user confirm submission of the order before any external payment.

core_features: Load and display a complete, read-only summary of a ticket order using getTicketOrderReview based on the order_id URL parameter.
Show event information associated with the ticket order, including title, timing, and venue details returned from getTicketOrderReview.
Display an itemized list of tickets in the order, including seat details and price tier information, by combining TicketOrderItem, Seat, and TicketPriceTier records from getTicketOrderReview.
Present an order totals section with subtotal, any fees, and total amount from the TicketOrder object returned by getTicketOrderReview.
Provide an 'Edit Details' action that navigates back to checkout.html while preserving the current order_id in the URL parameters.
Provide a 'Confirm Order' action that calls confirmTicketOrderSubmission with the current orderId to finalize the order before any external payment.
Handle and display success and error messages from confirmTicketOrderSubmission so the user knows whether the order was successfully submitted.
Enforce read-only behavior of the order details on this page; any changes to buyer or ticket details are only possible by navigating back to checkout.html.
Adapt available actions based on order.status (for example, enable confirmation only when the order is in_review and show informational messaging if it is already submitted or cancelled).
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing Order Review after Checkout
1. User completes buyer information on checkout.html and submits.
2. checkout.html creates or updates a TicketOrder with status set to in_review and obtains its TicketOrder.id.
3. checkout.html redirects the user to order_review.html with a URL parameter order_id set to this TicketOrder.id.
4. On page load, the Order Review page reads the order_id URL parameter.
5. If order_id is present, the page calls getTicketOrderReview with orderId set to the value of order_id.
6. While waiting for the response, the page shows a loading state and hides order details and actions.
7. When getTicketOrderReview returns, the page binds the TicketOrder, TicketOrderItem, Event, Seat, and TicketPriceTier data to the order summary, itemized ticket list, and totals components.
8. If the order.status returned is in_review, the 'Confirm Order' and 'Edit Details' actions are enabled; otherwise, they are disabled or hidden, and an informational status message is displayed instead.

Workflow: Editing Order Details from Order Review
1. User is on order_review.html with an order in in_review status and sees their order summary.
2. User clicks the 'Edit Details' button.
3. The page reads the current order_id from the URL and performs a navigation to checkout.html, appending the same order_id value as a URL parameter (checkout.html?order_id={current_order_id}).
4. checkout.html uses the provided order_id to load the existing order and associated cart, allowing the user to edit buyer information or ticket quantities.
5. After editing and resubmitting on checkout.html, the user is redirected again to order_review.html?order_id={same_or_updated_order_id} to review the updated order.

Workflow: Confirming Order Submission
1. User reviews their order details on order_review.html with an order in in_review status.
2. User clicks the 'Confirm Order' button.
3. The page reads the current order_id from the URL and calls confirmTicketOrderSubmission with orderId set to that value.
4. While waiting for the response, the page enters a submitting state: it disables the 'Confirm Order' button, optionally disables 'Edit Details', and shows a processing indicator.
5. If confirmTicketOrderSubmission returns success = true, the page updates its local order state to reflect the returned status (for example, submitted) and shows a success message to the user; the 'Confirm Order' and 'Edit Details' actions remain disabled to prevent duplicate submission or editing.
6. If confirmTicketOrderSubmission returns success = false, the page displays the returned message as an error, exits the submitting state, and re-enables the 'Confirm Order' button so the user may try again or choose to navigate back to checkout.

Workflow: Directly Visiting Order Review via URL
1. A user or staff member navigates directly to order_review.html with an order_id query parameter pointing to an existing TicketOrder (for example, via a bookmarked link or internal tool).
2. On load, the page reads order_id from the URL.
3. If order_id is present, the page calls getTicketOrderReview(orderId = order_id) and loads the returned data.
4. If the returned order.status is in_review, the page behaves the same as after checkout, allowing confirmation or editing.
5. If the order.status is submitted or cancelled, the page still displays the full read-only order summary but does not allow confirmation; the 'Confirm Order' button is either disabled or hidden, and an informational banner indicates that the order has already been processed or cancelled.
6. If getTicketOrderReview fails or returns no order, the page shows an error state and does not display confirm or edit actions.

Workflow: Handling Missing or Invalid order_id
1. User lands on order_review.html without an order_id parameter in the URL, or with an invalid value.
2. On initialization, the page detects that order_id is missing or invalid.
3. The page does not call getTicketOrderReview and instead shows an error message indicating that no order could be found to review.
4. The page disables or omits the 'Confirm Order' and 'Edit Details' actions because there is no valid order in context.
5. If site-wide navigation or links back to event pages or checkout are available, the user may use them to start a new checkout process (handled outside this page's interfaces).
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads the order_id query parameter from the URL and, if present, triggers a call to getTicketOrderReview with orderId = order_id.
If getTicketOrderReview is in progress, user interactions that depend on loaded order data (such as 'Confirm Order' and 'Edit Details') are disabled until the call resolves.

If getTicketOrderReview returns successfully, the page binds the returned TicketOrder, TicketOrderItem, Event, Seat, and TicketPriceTier objects to the relevant components (order header, items list, and totals).
If getTicketOrderReview returns an error or no order, the page shows an error message state and disables order-related actions.
Clicking the 'Edit Details' button triggers a navigation to checkout.html with the current order_id appended as a query parameter, allowing the user to modify the in-review order.
Clicking the 'Confirm Order' button, when enabled and when an order_id is present, triggers a call to confirmTicketOrderSubmission with orderId set from the URL.

While confirmTicketOrderSubmission is pending, the 'Confirm Order' button is disabled and may visually indicate a loading or processing state; the user cannot trigger a second confirmation until the first attempt finishes.

When confirmTicketOrderSubmission resolves with success = true, the page displays a success message using the returned message and updates the internal representation of order.status to the returned status; further confirmation attempts are prevented.

When confirmTicketOrderSubmission resolves with success = false, the page displays the returned message as an error and re-enables the 'Confirm Order' button, allowing the user to retry or return to checkout.

If order.status is not in_review when the data is loaded (for example, submitted or cancelled), the 'Confirm Order' button remains disabled or hidden, the 'Edit Details' action is disabled or hidden, and a status banner informs the user that the order can no longer be changed or resubmitted.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by the order_id URL parameter and the results of the assigned interfaces. On initialization, the page parses the order_id from the query string (e.g., order_review.html?order_id=...). If order_id is missing or empty, the page enters a 'no-order' error state and does not call getTicketOrderReview. If order_id is present, the page enters a 'loading' state and calls getTicketOrderReview with orderId set to this value. While 'loading', order content and actions dependent on order data are disabled. If getTicketOrderReview succeeds, the page stores the returned TicketOrder, TicketOrderItem, Event, Seat, and TicketPriceTier objects in its in-memory state and transitions to a 'ready' state. Within 'ready', additional derived states are determined by order.status: when status is in_review, both 'Edit Details' and 'Confirm Order' are available; when status is submitted or cancelled, actions that change the order are disabled or hidden, and a read-only view is shown. When the user triggers 'Confirm Order', the page enters a 'submitting' state and calls confirmTicketOrderSubmission(orderId). While 'submitting', further confirmation attempts are blocked. On a successful response (success = true), the page updates its internal order.status to the returned status, displays a success notice, and transitions to a 'submitted' state where editing and confirmation are no longer allowed. On a failure response (success = false), the page stays in 'ready' state but records an error message for display and re-enables the 'Confirm Order' action.
