At all times, the canonical source of which order is being displayed remains the order_id URL parameter; if navigation changes the URL (for example, to checkout.html?order_id=...), the receiving page uses that parameter to continue the workflow.

components.functionality: Central component responsible for reading the order_id from the URL, determining whether to call getTicketOrderReview, storing the loaded order-related data (TicketOrder, TicketOrderItem, Event, Seat, TicketPriceTier), managing high-level page states (no-order, loading, ready, submitted, error), and passing the appropriate data and flags to child components.
Invokes getTicketOrderReview when a valid order_id is present, handles loading and error states for that call, and returns the combined result object to the state manager.

Displays contextual status messages to the user based on current page state and order.status, such as loading messages, error messages when no order is found, notices when the order has already been submitted or cancelled, and confirmation of successful submission.

Shows high-level information about the order and the associated event in a read-only format: event title, event date and time, venue information from the Event and associated Location data provided via Event fields, and buyer details (name and email) from TicketOrder.

Renders an itemized listing of each ticket in the order by iterating over TicketOrderItem records and joining them with Seat and TicketPriceTier data. Each row can display the seat_label (or a synthesized label using seat.row and seat.seat_number), section information if available, and the unit_price from TicketOrderItem, along with any grouping by price tier if desired.

Displays monetary totals for the order in a read-only manner, including subtotal_amount, fees_amount (if present), and total_amount from the TicketOrder object. It also reflects the currency indirectly as provided by the related TicketPriceTier records or the business rules for display.

Provides core actions for this page: an 'Edit Details' control that navigates back to checkout.html retaining the current order_id, and a 'Confirm Order' control that initiates order submission via confirmTicketOrderSubmission. The visibility and enabled/disabled state of these controls depend on the current page state and order.status.

Handles the interaction with confirmTicketOrderSubmission. When triggered, it reads the current order_id from the URL, calls confirmTicketOrderSubmission(orderId), manages submitting and post-submit states, and passes success or error outcomes back to the state manager and status banner.

Displays appropriate content when no valid order_id is present or when getTicketOrderReview fails (such as a message indicating that there is no order to review and optionally providing links or guidance handled outside of this page's interfaces). It is shown instead of the main order content when the state manager is in a no-order or error state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: All order review elements are arranged in a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header (logo + nav + social). Within this main column, components are stacked vertically in a linear sequence: (1) status banner (if present), (2) order header summary acting as the primary title band, (3) order items list as the main body content, (4) order totals summary as a concise financial recap, and (5) order actions bar as the final decision area. Routing, loading, submission, and empty/error states control what appears in this column but do not introduce additional layout regions.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All order review elements are arranged in a single, centered content column beneath the existing full-width site header (logo + nav + social). Within this main column, components are stacked vertically in a linear sequence: (1) status banner (if present), (2) order header summary acting as the primary title band, (3) order items list as the main body content, (4) order totals summary as a concise financial recap, and (5) order actions bar as the final decision area. Routing, loading, submission, and empty/error states control what appears in this column but do not introduce additional layout regions.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered column roughly 960–1140px wide on desktop, leaving generous white margins on both sides. The order header summary section is given the most vertical breathing room (top and bottom margins ~48–64px) to echo the mockup’s large title band. The items list and totals summary form a visually unified middle block with comfortable but tighter spacing (~24–32px between them). The actions bar sits at the bottom with a noticeable top margin (~48px) to feel like a separate, contemplative decision area. The status banner is slimmer, with modest vertical height and ~16–24px padding, spanning the full width of the content column just below the global page header.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low. Each major section uses ample white space and minimal decoration, with 16–24px internal padding in cards/blocks and 24–32px gaps between closely related sections. Text lines are not crowded; lists and totals are presented in a clean, editorial style with clear separation between rows. Buttons and interactions are visually calm, with no large blocks of dense UI controls, preserving a formal, nonprofit aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is strongly top-down. After the global header, the eye moves first to any status banner (e.g., 'Review your order'), then to the order header summary, which acts as the main page title zone (event name, date, order ID). From there, attention naturally drops to the items list as the core content, continues to the totals summary where the financial impact is clarified, and finally rests on the actions bar where the user decides to edit or confirm. Within each block, alignment is mostly left with subtle right-aligned key figures (e.g., totals), echoing a classical editorial layout. State changes (loading, submission, errors) are expressed within this same vertical flow, typically centered in the column, without breaking the calm hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s full-width header, a single, centered content column floats in a field of white space, evoking a printed concert program. The top of this column may begin with a slim, full-width status banner, followed by a spacious band that functions as the primary title area for the order details. Below that, the ticket items occupy the center of the page as a simple, readable list, framed by generous margins that keep the layout airy. The totals summary sits just beneath the items, aligned within the same column but visually tightened to focus the eye on the financial figures. Finally, a quiet but clear actions bar anchors the bottom of the column, giving the user room to pause and decide between editing their order or confirming it. Invisible routing, loading, and submission components orchestrate which of these sections show, but everything remains within this calm, top-to-bottom editorial flow, with no fixed toolbars or app-like chrome intruding on the classical, nonprofit character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-review-router acts as a non-visual wrapper for the entire main content area beneath the global site header. It defines a centered content column (approximately 960–1140px wide) with auto side margins and a generous top margin of about 64px from the bottom of the header. All visible child components (status banner, summary, items, totals, actions, and empty/error views) are stacked vertically within this column in the defined order. The router controls which high-level view is rendered (normal review vs. empty/error) but does not add any visible borders or backgrounds of its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container in normal document flow; governs conditional rendering within a centered, max-width main column but does not itself appear visually.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the order-review-loader occupies the same centered main column as the other components. It displays a simple, understated loading indicator centered both horizontally and vertically within the column’s available viewport height (e.g., a serif 'Loading your order…' line and a subtle spinner). There is generous whitespace around the indicator, with no additional chrome, echoing the minimalist, editorial feel. It temporarily replaces the normal content stack (status, summary, items, totals, actions) so that the user sees a single, calm loading state in the middle of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the main content stack within the centered column; remains in normal flow and scrolls with the page if height exceeds the viewport, but by design usually fits into one viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-status-banner appears at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the global site header and the router’s top margin. It spans the full width of the centered column (approximately 960–1140px) and has a modest vertical height: about 16–24px padding top and bottom with no more than 1–2 lines of text. The text is left-aligned, using a readable serif or serif-like style, and any background color is muted and light, maintaining a dignified, nonprofit aesthetic. A margin of roughly 24px separates the bottom of the banner from the order-header-summary, ensuring it reads as a distinct but unobtrusive preface to the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main column; does not stick or float. It appears or disappears based on status but does not affect the full-width global header above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-header-summary is positioned immediately below the status banner (or directly below the router’s top margin when no banner is present), and it occupies the primary visual band at the top of the content column. It spans the full width of the centered column, with internal padding of about 24px on all sides. Vertically, it has generous top and bottom margins of around 48–64px relative to the surrounding sections, echoing the mockup’s large title area. Within this block, the event name or 'Order Review' title is prominently set near the top, left-aligned but visually centered within the white space; secondary details such as date, venue, order number, and purchaser information follow beneath in a restrained typographic hierarchy. Borders are minimal or extremely light (e.g., a fine bottom rule) to keep the feel open and editorial. This block sets the tone and context before the user’s eye moves down to the order items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block in normal flow; scales in height based on content but maintains generous vertical margins to preserve its role as the primary title band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-items-list sits directly beneath the order-header-summary, separated by a vertical gap of about 32px. It occupies the full width of the centered column and forms the main body of the page, analogous to the hero area in the mockup but filled with structured content instead of an image. Internally, the list has about 16–24px padding and uses a simple single-column layout: each ticket or line item is presented as a horizontal row, with descriptive text (e.g., section, row, seat, ticket type) left-aligned and key numerical values (quantity, line total) subtly right-aligned within the same width, maintaining clarity without crowding. Rows are separated by 8–16px of vertical space or thin dividing rules, and the entire block has comfortable whitespace above and below so it does not feel dense. The items-list’s visual height will typically be the largest section on the page, but the generous line spacing and padding keep it light and readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding section that grows with the number of items; remains a single column and scrolls naturally if the list becomes longer than the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-totals-summary is positioned directly beneath the items list, with a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to signal a transition from detailed content to financial summary. It spans the same full width of the centered column but is notably shorter in height—typically 1–3 lines of breakdown plus a prominent total. The block has 16–24px internal padding. Line items like 'Subtotal', 'Fees', and 'Discounts' are aligned in a two-column style: labels left, amounts right, keeping lots of whitespace between them. The grand total is placed at the bottom of the block, right-aligned and slightly more prominent (e.g., larger or bolder text), but the overall treatment remains clean and restrained. A subtle top border or increased spacing from the items list may be used to visually separate this summary without adding heavy boxes, preserving the editorial character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in normal flow, pinned immediately after the items list; height adapts to the number of total lines but generally remains compact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-actions-bar is the final visible section in the main column, sitting below the totals summary with a generous top margin of about 48px to create a contemplative pause before the decision. It spans the full width of the centered column but is visually low in height, primarily containing one horizontal row of buttons. On desktop, the bar aligns the primary 'Confirm Order' button towards the right edge of the column, with the secondary 'Return to Checkout' link/button to its left, maintaining roughly 12–16px horizontal spacing between controls. The overall bar has about 16px vertical padding to give the buttons breathing room without forming a heavy footer-like block. Background remains white or very light, with no sticky or fixed positioning; the bar feels like the natural end of the page’s reading flow rather than an application toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky action section at the bottom of the main column. On narrow viewports, buttons may wrap to a vertical stack, centered or left-aligned, but the bar always remains in normal document flow and scrolls off-screen as the user moves.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-confirmation-handler has no dedicated visual block of its own; instead, it modifies the state of existing components during submission. When the user initiates confirmation from the order-actions-bar, this handler can trigger a subtle, full-column overlay or dimming effect: a semi-transparent white layer over the main column with a centered, small 'Processing your order…' message, preserving the centered column width and generous whitespace. Alternatively, if no overlay is used, the primary confirm button enters a 'loading' state while the rest of the layout remains unchanged. In both cases, the spatial arrangement of the page is preserved—the handler simply adds a light, centered status element within the same column, ensuring the user’s focus stays on the main vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that, when active, may introduce a temporary, column-contained overlay or inline loading state; it does not alter the structure or positioning of the underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When invoked, the order-empty-or-error-view replaces the standard stack of status banner, header summary, items list, totals, and actions within the main centered column. It presents a single, calm message block roughly centered in the visible portion of the column, both horizontally and with generous top and bottom margins (around 64px or more from the global header and footer content). This block spans the full column width with about 24px internal padding and contains a brief serif headline (e.g., 'No order found' or 'There was a problem loading your order') and optional supporting text or a link to navigate back. The layout echoes the page’s title band: lots of white space surrounding a small amount of text, with no heavy borders or distracting UI, reinforcing the editorial, dignified feel even in error states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the normal main content stack within the centered column; remains static and scrolls normally, but typically fits within a single viewport for clarity.

name: Volunteer Opportunities
title: Volunteer Opportunities
description: Browse, filter, and sign up for volunteer shifts, including event support and usher/front-of-house roles, with on-page registration.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options for volunteer opportunities (role category, month, day of week, location) using getVolunteerFilterOptions.
List volunteer opportunities filtered by selected role category, year/month, day of week, and location using listVolunteerOpportunities.
Allow quick identification and optional filtering/highlighting of opportunities related to usher/front-of-house work based on opportunity titles and tags.
Display a detailed view for a selected volunteer opportunity (including date, time range, duration, status, and responsibilities) using getVolunteerOpportunityDetails.

Provide an embedded sign-up form for the selected opportunity that collects volunteer first name, last name, email, phone, and an optional consent to receive volunteer updates, submitting via registerForVolunteerOpportunity.
Maintain filters and selected opportunity state through URL parameters so that views can be shared and restored (role_category, year, month, day_of_week, location_id, opportunity_id).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Discover volunteer shifts by filters:
1. User navigates to volunteer.html (with or without URL parameters).
2. Page reads role_category, year, month, day_of_week, location_id from the URL if present.
3. Page calls getVolunteerFilterOptions to populate dropdowns for role category, month, day of week, and location.
4. Filter controls are initialized using URL values where available; otherwise defaults are derived from the returned options.
5. Page calls listVolunteerOpportunities with the current filter state.
6. User adjusts filters (role category, month, day of week, location, include-full toggle), which updates the URL query parameters and triggers a new listVolunteerOpportunities call.
7. User scans the resulting list, with any usher/front-of-house-related opportunities visually distinguished or optionally filtered client-side.

Workflow 2 – View details for a specific opportunity (e.g., usher/front-of-house):
1. User clicks on an opportunity in the list (e.g., one whose title or tags indicate usher/front-of-house duties).
2. The click sets opportunity_id in the URL via the URL state manager.
3. The page calls getVolunteerOpportunityDetails with the selected opportunityId.
4. A detail panel or section shows the opportunity’s formatted date, time range, duration (from duration_hours or computed), location, responsibilities, and remaining capacity.
5. If the opportunity status or remaining capacity indicates it is no longer open, the sign-up form within the detail view is disabled with an explanatory message.

Workflow 3 – Direct link to a specific opportunity:
1. User follows a link to volunteer.html with an opportunity_id parameter (and optionally role_category, year, month, day_of_week, location_id).
2. The page initializes filters from the URL and calls getVolunteerFilterOptions, then listVolunteerOpportunities so the list context matches the link.
3. In parallel or after list load, the page calls getVolunteerOpportunityDetails with the opportunity_id.
4. The detail view for that specific opportunity opens automatically, with the sign-up form ready if the opportunity is open.
5. Closing the detail view removes opportunity_id from the URL and returns focus to the list, preserving the current filters.

Workflow 4 – Register for a volunteer shift on-site:
1. Within the open opportunity detail view, the user fills in first name, last name, email, phone, and an optional checkbox to receive volunteer updates.
2. On form submission, client-side validation checks required fields and confirms the opportunity_id matches the currently selected opportunity.
3. The page calls registerForVolunteerOpportunity with opportunityId and the form fields (volunteerFirstName, volunteerLastName, email, phone, receiveUpdates).
4. While awaiting the response, the form shows a loading state and prevents duplicate submissions.
5. If the API returns success=true, the form shows a confirmation message and may reset or lock the fields; the page can optionally refresh getVolunteerOpportunityDetails to update remaining capacity.
6. If success=false or an error occurs, the form displays the returned message and allows the user to correct any issues and resubmit.

Workflow 5 – Identify only usher/front-of-house roles:
1. User toggles an "Usher & Front-of-House only" filter or similar control above the list.
2. The page applies a client-side filter to the current listVolunteerOpportunities result, showing only opportunities whose title or tags indicate usher or front-of-house duties (e.g., tags containing known keys or title containing those words), without additional API calls.
3. Toggling this control off restores the full filtered list from the existing result set.
interactions: Filter selection: Changing role category, month, day of week, or location updates internal filter state, writes the corresponding query parameters (role_category, year, month, day_of_week, location_id) to the URL, and triggers a call to listVolunteerOpportunities.

Include-full toggle: Toggling inclusion of full shifts updates an internal flag, optionally a query parameter (e.g., include_full), and triggers a call to listVolunteerOpportunities with includeFull set accordingly.

Usher/front-of-house focus: Toggling a local "Usher & Front-of-House only" control applies a client-side filter on the current opportunity list based on VolunteerOpportunity.title and VolunteerOpportunity.tags; no URL or API calls are required for this local view change.

Opportunity selection: Clicking a "View details" or similar action on an opportunity card sets opportunity_id in the URL, updates selected-opportunity state, and calls getVolunteerOpportunityDetails for that id.
Detail view close: Closing the detail panel clears the selected-opportunity state, removes opportunity_id from the URL, and focuses back on the list (without reloading filters).
Sign-up form input: Typing in text fields and toggling the updates checkbox updates local form state only; no API is called until submit.

Sign-up form submit: Submitting the volunteer registration form triggers client-side validation and then calls registerForVolunteerOpportunity; based on the response, the component updates its UI to show success, error, or validation feedback.

URL navigation/back-forward: When the browser back/forward buttons are used, the URL state manager reads the updated query parameters, reinitializes filters and selected opportunity, refetches the list via listVolunteerOpportunities if needed, and conditionally calls getVolunteerOpportunityDetails if an opportunity_id is present.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page parses the URL query parameters role_category, year, month, day_of_week, location_id, and opportunity_id. It then calls getVolunteerFilterOptions to obtain valid filter options. For each filter, if the corresponding parameter is present and matches one of the returned options (e.g., role_category matches a role_categories.key, day_of_week matches a day_of_week_options.key, location_id matches a Location.id, year/month matches one of the month_options entries), that value is set as the active filter; otherwise, a default is chosen based only on the API data (for example, the first available month option). With these resolved filter values, the page calls listVolunteerOpportunities, mapping URL parameters to the interface parameter names (role_category → roleCategory, day_of_week → dayOfWeek, location_id → locationId, year → year, month → month). The includeFull argument is derived from an internal toggle (optionally mapped from a query parameter such as include_full if present). The list results are stored in state for rendering and for any client-side usher/front-of-house filtering. If an opportunity_id exists in the URL, the page sets that as the selected opportunity and calls getVolunteerOpportunityDetails(opportunityId) to populate the detail view. Any change in filters or selected opportunity initiated by the user updates the URL query string via history.replaceState or history.pushState to keep the URL shareable. The URL state manager listens to popstate events so that browser back/forward updates filters and the selected opportunity, refetching listVolunteerOpportunities and getVolunteerOpportunityDetails as needed. No persistent state is stored outside the URL and in-memory page state; localStorage or other persistent client storage is not used.

components.functionality: Parses and manages URL query parameters (role_category, year, month, day_of_week, location_id, opportunity_id, and optionally include_full) and synchronizes them with the page’s internal filter state and selected opportunity.

Displays and manages filter controls for role category, month, day of week, location, and an include-full toggle. Initializes its options using getVolunteerFilterOptions and current filter values from the URL state, and triggers reloading of the opportunity list when filters change.

Provides a control to focus the list on usher and front-of-house opportunities by applying a client-side filter on the currently loaded opportunities, based on the VolunteerOpportunity.title and VolunteerOpportunity.tags fields.

Renders the list of volunteer opportunities returned from listVolunteerOpportunities, applies any local usher/front-of-house-only filter, and passes each opportunity to individual opportunity-card components. Handles loading and empty states.

Displays summary information for a single VolunteerOpportunity (title, role_category label, date/time summary derived from start_datetime and end_datetime, location name if available, status, and capacity indicators). Provides a control to open the detail view for that opportunity.

Shows detailed information for the currently selected opportunity by calling getVolunteerOpportunityDetails with the opportunity_id from URL state. Displays formatted date, time range, duration, location details, responsibilities/description, status, capacity/remaining_capacity, and embeds the volunteer-signup-form when appropriate.

Embedded within the opportunity detail view, this form collects volunteer_first_name, volunteer_last_name, email, phone, and an optional receive_updates flag for the selected opportunity and submits them via registerForVolunteerOpportunity. It manages validation, loading, success, and error states. The form is disabled or hidden if the opportunity is not open or has no remaining capacity.

Displays the capacity state of an opportunity (e.g., open, full, closed, cancelled) based on VolunteerOpportunity.status, capacity, registered_count, and remaining_capacity from getVolunteerOpportunityDetails. Influences whether the volunteer-signup-form is enabled.

Centralizes display of loading spinners and error messages for calls to getVolunteerFilterOptions, listVolunteerOpportunities, getVolunteerOpportunityDetails, and registerForVolunteerOpportunity, so that the UI consistently reflects asynchronous states.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered main content column beneath the existing full-width site header. Within that column, the order from top to bottom is: (1) page title band and optional hero image (consistent with the mockup, assumed to be shared across pages), (2) the volunteer-filters-bar as a horizontal control band spanning most of the column width, (3) the usher-foh-filter-toggle directly associated with the filters and aligned beneath/within that band, (4) the volunteer-opportunity-list as the primary content block, with each volunteer-opportunity-card stacked vertically, (5) when a card is selected, a volunteer-opportunity-detail section expands directly below that card in the same column, containing the capacity-status-indicator near the top of the detail and the volunteer-signup-form in its lower portion. Non-visual infrastructure components (url-state-manager, loading-and-error-handler) operate around these blocks without changing the core stacking order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered main content column beneath the existing full-width site header. Within that column, the order from top to bottom is: (1) page title band and optional hero image (consistent with the mockup, assumed to be shared across pages), (2) the volunteer-filters-bar as a horizontal control band spanning most of the column width, (3) the usher-foh-filter-toggle directly associated with the filters and aligned beneath/within that band, (4) the volunteer-opportunity-list as the primary content block, with each volunteer-opportunity-card stacked vertically, (5) when a card is selected, a volunteer-opportunity-detail section expands directly below that card in the same column, containing the capacity-status-indicator near the top of the detail and the volunteer-signup-form in its lower portion. Non-visual infrastructure components (url-state-manager, loading-and-error-handler) operate around these blocks without changing the core stacking order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All interactive content sits inside a centered content container roughly 960–1140px wide, never touching the viewport edges. The volunteer-filters-bar occupies a shallow but full-width-in-column band at the top of the main content, with comfortable vertical padding (~16–24px). A generous vertical gap (~48px) separates the title/hero region from the filters, and similar section spacing separates the filters from the list. The volunteer-opportunity-list occupies the bulk of the visible vertical space, with each card using moderate vertical height but generous separation (24–32px) between cards. The detail panel, when open, occupies roughly the same width as the cards but expands vertically as needed, with substantial padding (24px) and spacing above and below it to maintain the airy editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density remains low and calm. Controls in the filter bar are spaced with clear gaps (at least 16–24px between groups) and do not crowd edge-to-edge. Cards are not compact: each includes comfortable internal padding (~16–24px) and breathing room between text lines. The detail panel and signup form are spacious, with distinct vertical groupings of information separated by 16–24px. No element feels squeezed; white space is used liberally around and between sections to preserve a formal, editorial tone appropriate for a nonprofit choral organization.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves vertically in a clear sequence: header → page title → hero image (if present) → filter controls → list of opportunities → detail and signup for a selected opportunity. Within the filter bar, attention flows left-to-right from primary filters to the usher/front-of-house toggle. Within each card and detail panel, the capacity-status-indicator appears near the top so users immediately understand availability before committing to reading details or opening the signup form. The signup form comes last in the vertical flow, reinforcing the editorial read-then-act pattern. Any loading or error messaging appears centered over or within the main content column so the user’s gaze does not have to shift away from this central vertical path.

layout.overall_description: The Volunteer Opportunities page reads like a clean, editorial listing within a classical nonprofit site. Below the full-width site header and the large, generously spaced page title band, the main content is contained in a single, centered column framed by wide white margins on both sides. At the top of this column, a low-profile filter bar stretches across roughly 80–90% of the content width, aligned to the center, with neatly spaced filters and an usher/front-of-house toggle arranged horizontally. A comfortable vertical gap separates this controls band from the main list, which consists of vertically stacked volunteer opportunity cards, each occupying the full width of the content column but surrounded by white space so they feel like individual program entries rather than an app-like grid. When you click a card, it gracefully reveals a detail panel directly underneath, still within the same centered column, with the capacity status prominently near the top and a spacious signup form beneath. The entire layout emphasizes vertical reading and calm decision-making: every section is clearly separated by generous white space, typography is given room to breathe, and nothing crowds the edges, creating a dignified, approachable experience consistent with a traditional arts nonprofit.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and sits conceptually behind the main content column. It does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. Its influence is reflected in which filters are active, which opportunity card (if any) has its detail panel expanded, and whether the signup form is scrolled into view. Visually, the layout remains unchanged; only the state of other components (filter values, selected card, open detail) reflects the URL state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered infrastructure component with no visible footprint; it affects the state and visibility of other components without changing the spatial structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The volunteer-filters-bar is positioned near the top of the centered main content column, directly below the page title/hero region with a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px above it. It appears as a single horizontal band that spans roughly 80–90% of the content width and is centered within the container, leaving equal white margins on the left and right. Inside the bar, filter controls are arranged from left to right, starting near the left edge of the bar but still buffered by internal padding of about 16–24px. The bar itself has a subtle background difference (e.g., very light gray or a thin bottom rule) to distinguish it from the plain white page while still feeling understated. There is at least 16–24px of vertical padding inside the bar so that controls do not feel cramped, and 24–32px of space below the bar before the content below starts, preserving the airy editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally scrolls with the page; on larger desktops it may optionally become sticky only within the main content column once the user scrolls past the title, remaining fixed near the top of the viewport with the same centered width so that filtering remains accessible without breaking the centered, columnar composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The usher-foh-filter-toggle is visually integrated with the volunteer-filters-bar rather than isolated. It appears on the right half of the filter bar’s horizontal row, aligned to the right edge of the internal content area within the bar. There is a clear horizontal gap (at least 24px) between it and the nearest filter control group to its left, signaling that it is a specialized local filter. The toggle itself occupies a small fraction of the bar’s width (around 15–20%), but is given adequate horizontal padding so that its labels are readable in the serif type. Vertically, it is centered along the height of the filter bar, maintaining the clean, straight baseline of controls. On narrow screens, it can break onto a second line just below the main filters but still fully within the filter bar, with 8–16px vertical spacing separating it from the filters above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inside the volunteer-filters-bar; its position is responsive, sitting inline to the right on wide viewports and wrapping below other filters within the same bar on smaller widths, always aligned to the right edge of the bar’s internal content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The volunteer-opportunity-list sits directly below the volunteer-filters-bar, separated by a vertical gap of about 32–48px. It occupies the full width of the main content column, which is centered on the page and constrained to approximately 960–1140px. The list is purely vertical: each volunteer-opportunity-card is stacked one after the other with consistent spacing (24–32px) of white space between cards. There is no grid or multi-column layout; the list reads like a sequence of program entries on a single column editorial page. When a card is expanded to show its detail, the detail panel appears between that card and the next card while other cards above and below still maintain their original spacing, preserving the linear top-to-bottom reading order. The list’s bottom has a generous margin (48–64px) before any page footer content, ensuring the last card or detail panel doesn’t feel cramped at the bottom of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing block within the main column; it grows vertically with content. When loading or error states apply, it acts as the anchor region for overlays or inline messages from the loading-and-error-handler.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each volunteer-opportunity-card is a full-width block within the main content column, with left and right edges aligned to the main text column (roughly 80–90% of the page width, centered). The card has a subtle boundary—a light border or drop shadow is minimal or absent; emphasis is instead on spacing and a possible thin bottom rule. Internal padding of around 16–24px on all sides ensures a calm, readable layout for the title, date/time, location, and a short description. The card’s vertical height is moderate, typically occupying about 15–25% of the viewport height, but allowed to grow naturally with text, and each card is separated from the next by 24–32px of whitespace. The primary text (opportunity name) is set near the top-left of the card, with supporting details below in a clean, left-aligned stack. If a teaser for capacity is present on the card itself, it appears near the top-right corner inside the card, mirroring the header’s left-title/right-icons pattern, but remains subtle to preserve the formal aesthetic. Clicking or tapping the card does not shift it horizontally; instead, it triggers vertical expansion of the associated detail panel immediately beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as part of the vertical list; each card is a clickable block that, when activated, anchors its corresponding volunteer-opportunity-detail panel directly beneath it without altering the column alignment. On small screens, the card simply becomes taller but stays full-width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The volunteer-opportunity-detail appears directly below the volunteer-opportunity-card that triggered it, sharing the same horizontal boundaries as the card and staying fully within the main centered column. It is visually separated from the card above by a modest vertical gap (16–24px) and possibly a thin divider, and from the next card below by a larger gap (24–32px) to clearly group the detail with its card. The detail panel uses a generous internal padding of about 24px, making it feel like a focused, editorial article section devoted to that specific opportunity. It can occupy a substantial vertical portion of the page—anywhere from 30–60% of the viewport height depending on content—and includes distinct sub-sections: a top area with the full opportunity title and capacity-status-indicator aligned along the same horizontal line (title left, status right), a middle area with descriptive text and logistical details arranged in left-aligned blocks, and a lower area that transitions into or contains the volunteer-signup-form. All content is left-aligned with ample line spacing, and there’s clear visual hierarchy so users naturally read top-to-bottom before reaching the signup form at the bottom of the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands and collapses inline within the list. Only one detail panel is fully expanded at a time on desktop (optional behavior), closing others to maintain a manageable vertical flow. It scrolls with the page, respecting the centered column and never overlaying other content like a modal.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The volunteer-signup-form is embedded within the lower portion of the volunteer-opportunity-detail panel, occupying the same width as the detail content and adhering to the same left and right margins. A clear visual separation (such as a subtle top rule or a 24–32px vertical spacer and a section heading like “Sign Up”) distinguishes the form from the informational text above. The form fields are arranged in a single column to match the overall editorial flow, with each input stretching to roughly 60–80% of the detail panel width and left-aligned. Vertical spacing between fields is comfortable (16–24px), and grouped fields (e.g., first/last name) may appear side-by-side on wider screens while still respecting the main column margins. The submit button sits at the bottom-left of the form area, with at least 24px of whitespace below it before the bottom edge of the detail panel, so the form feels anchored but not cramped. Overall, the form occupies roughly the lower third of the detail panel, expanding vertically as needed for additional fields or validation messages.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as part of the volunteer-opportunity-detail content. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or float; however, focus management may scroll the column to ensure the form or a validation error is visible within the detail panel after submission attempts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The capacity-status-indicator is a compact, high-salience but visually restrained element that appears primarily within the volunteer-opportunity-detail panel, and optionally as a lighter version on the volunteer-opportunity-card. In the detail panel, it is positioned in the top-right corner of the content area, horizontally aligned with the opportunity title on the left. It occupies a small footprint—roughly 10–15% of the panel’s width—with either a pill-shaped label or concise text accompanied by a subtle icon. It is vertically centered within the first content row so that the user reads the title and almost simultaneously notes the status (e.g., “Open,” “Filling Fast,” “Full”). On the card, if used, it appears in a similar top-right position but with even more subtle styling, such as a thin outline and smaller type, so it does not overpower the card title. The indicator does not push other content out of alignment; instead, the text content wraps naturally beneath the title while the indicator nests in the upper-right corner within the card or detail panel boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the top-right of its containing block (card or detail panel), maintaining its position as the container resizes. It does not scroll independently or float; it moves with the panel’s top row when the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-handler manifests over or within the main content column where the list and filters sit. On initial page load or when filters change, a loading state appears centered horizontally within the column, roughly in the vertical space where the volunteer-opportunity-list would appear—just below the filters bar. This might be a simple typographic message (e.g., “Loading volunteer opportunities…”) or a subtle spinner, with generous whitespace around it so it feels integrated and not intrusive. For errors (such as network issues or empty results), the handler displays an inline message block full-width within the content column, aligned to the same left and right edges as the list. The error block has modest padding (16–24px) and a subtle border or background tint to distinguish it from normal content but still align with the understated aesthetic. It is separated from neighboring components above and below by 24–32px of whitespace. At no point does the handler cover the entire viewport; it remains visually anchored to the central column and respects the vertical flow between filters and list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the main content column, replacing or overlaying the volunteer-opportunity-list region as needed but never fixed to the viewport. During loading, interactions with the filters bar may be temporarily disabled or subtly dimmed while the handler is visible; as soon as loading completes, the handler collapses and the list reclaims the space.

name: Youth Choirs & Ensembles
title: Youth Choirs & Ensembles
description: Page for exploring youth ensembles, applying filters (age, season, tuition, schedule), viewing detailed ensemble information, comparing options, and starting on-site registration for a selected ensemble.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options (seasons, age ranges, rehearsal locations) using getEnsembleFilterOptions and use them to build the filter controls.
List ensembles using listEnsembles, filtered by age, season, maximum tuition, and latest acceptable rehearsal end time, with optional additional filters (e.g., weekday-only).
Support URL-driven initial state, pre-filtering ensembles using incoming parameters age, season_id, max_tuition, latest_end_time, and optionally opening a specific ensemble via ensemble_id.
Provide an ensemble detail view that loads full information for a selected ensemble (eligibility, rehearsal day and exact start/end times, tuition, rehearsal location) using getEnsembleDetails.

Allow users to quickly compare at least two ensembles by pinning them for comparison and switching the detail view between them, assisted by URL parameters that remember which ensembles are being compared.

Offer an in-page registration start flow from the ensemble detail view, collecting singer name, age, school, and season selection, and creating an EnsembleRegistration via startEnsembleRegistration without leaving the site.

Provide in-context navigation from an ensemble detail to the auditions page (auditions.html) with the singer’s age as a URL parameter, and to the scholarships page (scholarships.html) with the selected season as a URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Discover eligible ensembles from index.html:
1. User arrives on ensembles.html from index.html with one or more URL parameters (age, season_id, max_tuition, latest_end_time, optionally ensemble_id).
2. The page parses these parameters and calls getEnsembleFilterOptions to load season, age, and location options.
3. The filter bar initializes its controls using the URL parameters (e.g., pre-selecting age from age and season from season_id if provided).
4. The page calls listEnsembles with age, seasonId, maxTuition, latestEndTime, and other filter flags derived from the current filter state.
5. The ensemble list displays the filtered ensembles. If ensemble_id is present, the detail panel automatically opens for that ensemble using getEnsembleDetails.
6. The user may adjust filters (age, season, tuition, latest end time); each change updates the URL and triggers a new listEnsembles call and list refresh.

Workflow 2 – Open ensemble details and check eligibility/schedule/tuition:
1. From the filtered list, the user clicks an ensemble card or row.
2. The URL parameter ensemble_id is updated to the selected ensemble’s id.
3. The detail panel component calls getEnsembleDetails with ensembleId from the updated URL.
4. The panel displays full ensemble information: age and/or grade eligibility, rehearsal day, start and end time, tuition amount and currency, and rehearsal location.
5. The user can close the detail panel, which clears ensemble_id from the URL (or sets it to a neutral state) and returns focus to the list.

Workflow 3 – Compare two ensembles for schedule and tuition:
1. User browses the ensemble list; each ensemble card offers a control such as “Pin for comparison”.
2. When the user pins an ensemble, its id is added to a comparison set and encoded in a URL parameter (e.g., compare_ids containing a comma-separated list of up to two ensemble ids).
3. The comparison helper component uses the compare_ids parameter to show up to two pinned ensembles as quick-select buttons, using name and key summary data already available from the list or detail calls.
4. Clicking a pinned ensemble in the comparison helper updates ensemble_id in the URL and triggers getEnsembleDetails to show that ensemble in the detail panel.
5. The user can toggle between the pinned ensembles via the comparison helper to compare rehearsal end times against their acceptable latest_end_time constraint and see which ones fall under their current tuition filter.
6. Removing an ensemble from comparison updates compare_ids in the URL and hides it from the comparison helper.

Workflow 4 – Start registration for an ensemble:
1. In an ensemble detail view, the user selects a relevant season (if multiple seasons are available) or accepts the pre-selected season derived from the page’s filter context or ensemble data.
2. The user clicks a “Register” or equivalent button for that ensemble.
3. A registration stepper opens within the page, pre-filling ensemble and season context and optionally pre-filling age from the current filter or incoming age URL parameter.
4. The user completes Step 1 of the registration form: singer_first_name, singer_last_name, singer_age, singer_school, and confirms or changes the season selection.
5. On form submission, the stepper calls startEnsembleRegistration with ensembleId, seasonId, singerFirstName, singerLastName, singerAge, and singerSchool.
6. If the API returns success, the stepper stores the returned registration object in page state, may add a URL parameter registration_id with registration.id, and shows a confirmation message.
7. The stepper exposes a “Continue to next step” action that keeps the user on the site (for example, transitioning to a next-step panel within the same page), while keeping the registration context (ensembleId, seasonId, and registration.id) in memory and/or URL parameters.

Workflow 5 – Navigate to auditions and scholarships from an ensemble:
1. From an ensemble detail panel, the user clicks “Schedule an Audition”.
2. If the user has provided a singer age (through filter, registration form, or input field), that age is used as selected_singer_age in the outgoing navigation to auditions.html as the age URL parameter; the page navigates to auditions.html?age=selected_singer_age.
3. From the same detail view, the user can click “Need Tuition Assistance?”.
4. The currently selected season id (from the filter context, ensemble season, or registration stepper) is used as selected_season_id in the outgoing navigation; the page navigates to scholarships.html?season_id=selected_season_id.
5. Returning from auditions.html or scholarships.html using browser navigation or internal links preserves the ensembles.html URL parameters, maintaining the filter context and selected ensemble when possible.

Workflow 6 – Direct navigation and bookmarking:
1. A user or staff member copies the URL from ensembles.html when a particular filter set and ensemble detail are selected (including parameters such as age, season_id, max_tuition, latest_end_time, ensemble_id, and compare_ids).
2. Another user visits this URL directly (or the same user returns later).
3. On load, the URL state manager component reads all parameters and initializes filters, comparison pins, and the currently open ensemble accordingly.
4. The page calls getEnsembleFilterOptions, then listEnsembles with the appropriate parameters, and if ensemble_id is present, also calls getEnsembleDetails.
5. The result is a reproducible, shareable state: the same ensembles are listed, the same detail panel is open, and the same comparison set is restored, without relying on local storage.
interactions:
Filter interactions:
- Changing any filter (season dropdown, age selector, maximum tuition input, latest acceptable rehearsal end time input, optional weekday-only toggle, optional location filter) updates the internal filter state and synchronizes corresponding URL parameters.
- On filter change, the page calls listEnsembles with parameters derived from the new filter state and re-renders the ensemble list.
- Resetting filters clears related URL parameters (or sets them to default values) and triggers a fresh listEnsembles call.

Ensemble selection interactions:
- Clicking an ensemble in the list sets ensemble_id in the URL and triggers the detail panel to load data using getEnsembleDetails.
- Closing or dismissing the detail panel removes ensemble_id from the URL or sets it to an inactive value and returns focus to the list.
- Keyboard users can navigate to an ensemble entry and activate it to open the detail panel using the same handler.

Comparison interactions:
- Each ensemble card and the detail view include a control for adding/removing that ensemble from the comparison set.
- Adding an ensemble for comparison appends its id to the compare_ids URL parameter (respecting a maximum of two ids).
- If two ensembles are already in compare_ids, adding a third either replaces one existing id based on a defined rule (such as replacing the oldest) or is prevented with appropriate feedback.
- The comparison helper displays the ensembles represented by compare_ids and allows the user to click one to view its details, which updates ensemble_id in the URL and triggers getEnsembleDetails.
- Removing an ensemble from comparison updates compare_ids and refreshes the comparison helper state.

Registration interactions:
- Clicking a “Register” button in the ensemble detail panel opens the registration stepper and sets a registration mode flag in page state (and optionally adds a URL parameter such as registration_mode=1).
- Submitting Step 1 of the registration form triggers validation (e.g., required fields present, age numeric) and then calls startEnsembleRegistration.
- On a successful response, the registration stepper stores the returned registration object, updates the URL with registration_id and registration_ensemble_id, and displays success feedback.
- On an error response or failure, the stepper shows an error message and does not proceed to the next step.
- Clicking “Continue to next step” within the stepper changes the internal step index (and optionally updates a URL parameter such as registration_step=2) to display the subsequent panel while preserving registration context.
- Closing the stepper clears registration-specific UI state; if desired, registration-specific URL parameters are removed or left intact so that the flow can be restored from a bookmarked link.

Outgoing navigation interactions:
- When the user clicks “Schedule an Audition”, the handler determines a singer age in this priority order: explicit age provided in the registration stepper, age filter input, or incoming URL age parameter, if available; that numeric value is used as age in the query string for auditions.html.
- When the user clicks “Need Tuition Assistance?”, the handler determines the relevant season id from the registration stepper season field, the current filter season selection, or the ensemble’s seasonId; that id is used as season_id in the query string for scholarships.html.
- Both actions perform a standard navigation to the respective page with the appropriate URL parameters, without using any additional APIs from this page.

URL and state synchronization interactions:
- On initial page load, the URL-state manager parses age, season_id, max_tuition, latest_end_time, ensemble_id, compare_ids, and any registration-related parameters and initializes the relevant components.
- When components change these values (e.g., selecting an ensemble, updating filters, modifying comparison sets, starting registration), they emit events that the state manager uses to update the URL via the History API without a full reload.
- A full reload or direct navigation always reconstructs component state from the URL, ensuring no reliance on localStorage or other persistent client storage.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s primary state is fully derived from and synchronized to URL parameters, alongside transient in-memory UI flags.

Incoming URL parameters:
- age (number):
  - Used as the initial age filter value and passed to listEnsembles(age).
  - Used as the default age in the registration stepper’s singer_age field if the user has not entered a different age.
  - Used as selected_singer_age when navigating to auditions.html (if no more specific age has been entered).
- season_id (string):
  - Used as the initial selected season in the filter bar and passed to listEnsembles(seasonId).
  - Used as the default season selection in the registration stepper unless the user chooses another season from the available options.
  - Used as selected_season_id when navigating to scholarships.html (unless overridden by a registration-specific season selection).
- max_tuition (number):
  - Used as the initial maximum tuition filter value and passed to listEnsembles(maxTuition).
  - The ensemble list only displays ensembles whose tuition_amount satisfies this constraint according to the backend logic.
- latest_end_time (string, HH:MM 24-hour format):
  - Used as the initial latest rehearsal end time filter, passed to listEnsembles(latestEndTime).
  - Primarily influences which ensembles appear in the list; the detail panel still shows actual rehearsal end times for comparison against the user’s constraint.
- ensemble_id (string):
  - If present, triggers loading of that ensemble’s details via getEnsembleDetails(ensembleId).
  - Controls whether the detail panel is open and for which ensemble; changing ensemble_id changes the currently displayed ensemble.

Additional URL parameters used for internal state:
- compare_ids (string):
  - Represents a comma-separated list of up to two Ensemble.id values that the user has pinned for comparison.
  - The comparison helper parses this value to build its pinned list; modifying this list (e.g., adding or removing an ensemble) updates compare_ids.
- weekday_only (string/boolean representation, optional):
  - When present and truthy, corresponds to a “weekday-only rehearsals” filter toggle.
  - Passed to listEnsembles(weekdayOnly) when calling the interface.
- include_inactive (string/boolean representation, optional):
  - When present, may allow staff-facing views that include inactive ensembles by passing includeInactive to listEnsembles; typically absent or false for public users.
- registration_id (string, optional):
  - When present, indicates that a startEnsembleRegistration call previously succeeded for the current context.
  - Used by the registration stepper to restore the state of a previously started registration and show the appropriate success/next-step messaging.
- registration_ensemble_id (string, optional):
  - Tracks which ensemble the registration_id refers to; allows the registration stepper to verify that the currently displayed ensemble matches the stored registration context.
- registration_step (string/number, optional):
  - Indicates which step in the registration stepper should be shown (e.g., 1 for entering singer information, 2 for reviewing confirmation/next steps).
  - Changing this parameter (for example, upon clicking “Continue to next step”) updates the visible panel inside the registration component.

State reconstruction and transitions:
- On load, the URL-state manager:
  1) Reads all parameters listed above.
  2) Calls getEnsembleFilterOptions, then applies season_id, age, and other parameters to initialize the filter bar.
  3) Calls listEnsembles(age, seasonId, maxTuition, weekdayOnly, latestEndTime, includeInactive) with values derived from the URL.
  4) If ensemble_id is present, calls getEnsembleDetails(ensembleId) and opens the detail panel.
  5) If compare_ids is present, initializes the comparison helper with those ensembles.
  6) If registration_id (and optionally registration_ensemble_id and registration_step) are present, instructs the registration stepper to show the appropriate state.
- Any user action that changes these parameters (filter changes, ensemble selection, comparison updates, registration progress) triggers the state manager to update the URL and then re-notify relevant components as needed.
- All persistent state is encoded in the URL; in-memory state is treated as a temporary reflection of the URL and can be reconstructed after full reloads or navigation.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, parses and applies URL parameters, orchestrates calls to getEnsembleFilterOptions, listEnsembles, and getEnsembleDetails, and keeps the URL in sync with user actions across filters, ensemble selection, comparison, and registration steps.

Displays and manages ensemble filters including season, age, maximum tuition, latest acceptable rehearsal end time, and optional flags such as weekday-only or location. Initializes from URL parameters and emits filter-change events that trigger listEnsembles calls and URL updates.

Renders the list of ensembles returned by listEnsembles based on current filters. Supports selecting an ensemble to view details and pinning ensembles to the comparison helper. Reflects changes when filters are updated or when age/season/tuition/time constraints change.

Shows detailed information for the currently selected ensemble, including age and grade eligibility, rehearsal weekday and precise start/end times, tuition and currency, and rehearsal location details. Provides actions to start registration, schedule an audition, request tuition assistance, and add the ensemble to the comparison set.

Manages a small set of pinned ensembles used for comparison (up to two). Reads and writes the compare_ids URL parameter, displays pinned ensembles with summary information, and allows quick switching of the detail panel between them by updating ensemble_id.

Provides an in-page, two-phase registration start flow. Step 1 collects singer_first_name, singer_last_name, singer_age, singer_school, and seasonId for the selected ensemble and calls startEnsembleRegistration. Step 2 displays confirmation and guidance for next steps while preserving registration context, staying on the same site and optionally reflecting progress in URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header, large page title, and hero image, all functional blocks are stacked in a single, centered content column: first the ensembles filter bar, then a slim comparison helper band, followed by the vertical list of ensembles, then a full-width detail panel for the selected ensemble, and finally the registration stepper as the last, clearly separated section. Within this column, content is predominantly left-aligned text inside a visually centered container, preserving an editorial, page-like feel rather than an app-like dashboard.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header, large page title, and hero image, all functional blocks are stacked in a single, centered content column: first the ensembles filter bar, then a slim comparison helper band, followed by the vertical list of ensembles, then a full-width detail panel for the selected ensemble, and finally the registration stepper as the last, clearly separated section. Within this column, content is predominantly left-aligned text inside a visually centered container, preserving an editorial, page-like feel rather than an app-like dashboard.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The centered content column is constrained to roughly 960–1140px max-width and sits in the middle of a white page, with generous left/right margins on large screens. Major sections (filter bar, list, detail, registration) are separated by 48–64px of vertical space, while closely related elements (filter bar and comparison helper, individual cards in the list) use 16–32px spacing. The header and title area above these components retain significant vertical breathing room, so this functional area feels like the second half of a long, composed page rather than a cramped interface.
layout_strategies.content_density: Controls and text are spaced generously, with relatively few interactive elements per horizontal row. The filter bar shows only essential controls in a single line (wrapping on smaller screens); the ensemble list uses tall, readable cards with ample padding; the detail panel is laid out like a short article. Forms in the registration stepper use 24–32px vertical spacing between fields, preventing visual clutter. The result is low to medium density, prioritizing readability and calm over packing in options.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves top-down: site header and nav, then the page title and hero image, then into the functional column. Within this column, attention first lands on the filter bar as a subtle control band, then on the comparison helper just below, then down into the list of ensembles. After selecting an ensemble, the eye naturally continues downward into the dedicated detail section, and finally into the registration stepper at the bottom. Each band reads like a new section of a program or brochure, supporting a linear journey from exploration to decision to registration, with no competing sidebars or visual noise breaking the vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the dignified header, large serif page title, and centered hero image, a single, visually centered content column holds all youth-ensemble functionality. This column sits on a pure white background with generous margins on either side, evoking a printed program page. At the top of this column, a refined filter bar forms a thin horizontal band, left-aligning its label and controls while still sitting in the center of the overall page width. Directly beneath it, a quieter comparison helper line stretches across the same column, offering comparison affordances without heavy UI chrome. The main body is a vertical stack of ensemble cards, each full-width within the column and separated by generous white space, encouraging a calm, scroll-based browse. When an ensemble is selected, a substantial detail panel appears as the next full-width section down, framed by white space and light rules, like a feature article about that ensemble. At the bottom of the column, separated by a clear vertical break, the registration stepper occupies its own band, with a simple horizontal step indicator and a centered, comfortable form layout. The entire page reads top-to-bottom, with each section clearly delineated and resting in ample white space, maintaining the nonprofit’s traditional, editorial character while quietly supporting filtering, comparison, and registration tasks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no visual presence and does not occupy explicit space in the layout. It functions as an invisible layer that synchronizes the filter state, selected ensemble, comparison selections, and registration step with the URL. Conceptually, it is attached to the main content column that begins below the hero image, but it does not alter the spacing, alignment, or visual composition of any other component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely logical component that is loaded with the page and remains active throughout scrolling without affecting layout or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ensembles filter bar is the first functional element below the existing hero image. It is horizontally centered on the page and constrained to the main content column, spanning approximately 80–90% of the viewport width up to a max of about 960–1140px. There is a generous vertical gap (about 48–64px) between the bottom of the hero image and the top edge of this bar, so it reads as the beginning of a new, functional section. The filter bar itself is a low-profile horizontal band with 16–24px of vertical padding and a subtle bottom border or faint tinted background to distinguish it from the surrounding white without feeling heavy. Inside this bar, content is left-aligned: a small section label or heading (e.g., 'Filter ensembles') appears at the far left, with filter controls (age, season, tuition, schedule) arranged in a single row to the right, each control taking a modest, consistent width with roughly 16–24px horizontal gaps between them. On wide desktops, the controls form one clean line occupying about two-thirds to three-quarters of the column’s width; on narrower viewports, the controls wrap to a second line while maintaining left alignment and the same internal padding. The overall effect is a tidy, unobtrusive control strip that sits calmly at the top of the exploration area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow by default. On larger screens it may optionally become sticky to the top edge of the content column (just beneath the global header) once scrolled, but it remains confined to the centered column rather than stretching edge-to-edge, preserving the editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ensemble comparison helper sits directly below the filter bar within the same centered content column, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px. It spans the same width as the filter bar, roughly 80–90% of the viewport up to the column maximum, and aligns perfectly with it left and right, reinforcing the sense of a continuous, structured column. Visually, it is lighter and slimmer than the filter bar: a single line or two of text-sized elements with about 12–16px of vertical padding and no strong background fill. A short explanatory sentence, such as 'Select ensembles to compare,' is left-aligned, while comparison actions (e.g., a 'Compare' button and a simple count of selected ensembles) sit to the right within the same line or on the next line if the viewport is narrow. The helper feels like a caption or footer associated with the filters—informational and supportive rather than dominant. It visually recedes compared to the filters and list but anchors the comparison functionality at the top of the browsing experience so users understand its presence as they scroll through the ensembles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band in the vertical flow, scrolling away with the content. It does not stick or float; its role is to introduce and clarify comparison behavior rather than to act as a persistent control bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ensemble list occupies the main central section of the page and begins just below the comparison helper, with a comfortable vertical gap of about 32px to mark it as a distinct content area. Like the components above it, the list is constrained to the main centered content column, spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport up to the 960–1140px max width and aligned centrally on the page. Within this column, ensemble cards are stacked vertically in a single column, each card occupying the full available width of the column. Each card has a white background, a subtle 1px border or hairline rule, and internal padding of approximately 24px on all sides, with 24–32px of vertical spacing between cards. Text (ensemble name, age range, rehearsal schedule, tuition highlights) is left-aligned within the card, following the classical editorial feel—no heavy tiles or app-like blocks. Selection affordances (e.g., a 'View details' link or a checkbox for comparison) are aligned toward the right edge of each card but remain visually modest, relying on typography and subtle rules rather than colored buttons for hierarchy. The list can extend over multiple viewport heights; as the user scrolls, they experience a sequence of evenly spaced, readable cards in a calm, vertical rhythm. On smaller screens, the list maintains a single-column layout, only reducing horizontal padding and margins slightly to fit the viewport while preserving the stacked, editorial structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main page flow. Cards do not fix or float; the list simply extends downward, forming the core of the scroll experience between the filters above and the detail/registration sections below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ensemble detail panel appears as a distinct section below the ensemble list, separated from the last card by a clear vertical gap of about 48px, so it reads as the next major section in the page. It shares the same centered content column as the list, spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport up to the 960–1140px max width, and is horizontally aligned with all components above. When no ensemble is selected, this area may be absent or replaced by a minimal placeholder line that maintains the vertical rhythm without drawing attention. When an ensemble is selected, the detail panel becomes a substantial, article-like block with a white background, a slightly stronger top border or rule (possibly in an accent color), and generous internal padding of about 32px on all sides. The selected ensemble’s name appears at the top left in a larger serif heading, echoing the typographic hierarchy of the main page title but at a smaller scale. Below, the content is arranged in a mostly vertical flow: a short overview paragraph, followed by structured information such as rehearsal location, schedule, tuition, and audition requirements. On wider screens, these informational groups may be laid out in a subtle two-column grid within the panel (e.g., schedule and location on the left two-thirds, tuition and policies on the right third), but the overall impression remains that of a single, coherent block of text rather than a complex dashboard. The panel’s height is flexible, typically occupying about half to a full viewport height depending on content, and the user simply scrolls through it as part of the main page. The generous white margin above and below ensures it feels like a focused reading section devoted to that ensemble.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the scroll. It appears and disappears based on selection state but does not overlay or float; instead it pushes content down/up, maintaining the linear, book-like reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison helper is already described above as a slim band between the filter bar and the list. Functionally, it also connects visually to the ensemble list by reflecting the user’s in-card comparison selections (e.g., showing how many ensembles are selected) and may, when a comparison is initiated, offer a simple link or button to open a separate comparison view (if present elsewhere) or to emphasize differences in the list. Throughout, it remains visually understated and aligned with the editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, integrated within the content column, serving as an informational and control bridge between the filters and the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ensemble registration stepper is positioned at the bottom of the main content column, following the detail panel with a generous vertical separation of about 64px to signal a transition from exploration into commitment. It spans the same centered content width—approximately 80–90% of the viewport up to the 960–1140px max—maintaining alignment with all previous sections. The stepper is contained within a lightly defined panel: either a faintly tinted background or a thin surrounding border, with 24–32px of padding inside, distinguishing it from the pure-white content blocks above but keeping the look restrained and classical. At the top of this panel, a horizontal step indicator runs nearly the full width of the column: simple labeled steps (e.g., '1. Participant Info', '2. Guardian & Contact', '3. Review & Submit') laid out from left to right, with the current step indicated by a subtle typographic emphasis or a small underline, not heavy colored pills. Below the step indicator, the active step’s content is centered but slightly narrower than the full column (around 60–70% of the column width) to keep form fields within a comfortable reading line. Fields are arranged in one or two columns depending on screen size, with 24–32px vertical spacing between groups and ample white space around buttons at the bottom. Primary actions (Next, Submit) are right-aligned within this narrower form area, with secondary actions (Back, Cancel) in a lighter style to the left or below. The stepper’s overall height varies with content but typically occupies between two-thirds and one full viewport height, allowing most steps to fit comfortably without feeling cramped. The section reads as a calm, well-structured form at the end of a narrative page rather than a high-intensity transactional wizard.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, linear multi-step form. The content of the panel updates as the user moves between steps, but the panel itself remains in the same place in the vertical flow; it does not overlay the page or detach from the scroll.

name: Newsletter Signup
title: Newsletter Signup
description: Dedicated subscription page where users enter their contact details, choose newsletter topics and email frequency, agree to terms, and submit a newsletter subscription.

core_features: Load available newsletter interests and frequency options from the backend using getNewsletterOptions and bind them to the signup form.
Preselect newsletter interests and frequency based on incoming URL parameters (context, preselect_interests, frequency) and API-provided defaults.
Collect a required email address and required first and last name fields for newsletter subscription.
Optionally collect an organization name field without persisting it to the newsletter subscription API (reserved for future integrations).
Allow users to select and deselect specific newsletter interests returned by the API, enforcing at least one interest before submission.
Allow users to choose a single newsletter frequency, enforcing logic that prevents selection of daily or weekly options when monthly is chosen.
Require users to agree to terms or privacy policy via a checkbox before enabling form submission.
