component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows at the bottom of the main column; no fixed or sticky behavior. On smaller screens, it remains centered within the column, with the button expanding slightly in width if needed for readability.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for viewing contact information and sending general inquiries about programs, auditions, scholarships, volunteering, and events.

core_features: Load and display structured contact information (phone numbers, email addresses, mailing address, and locations) using getContactInfo.
Provide a general contact form that can be prefilled with a topic and subject from URL parameters and submits inquiries via submitContactForm.
List main locations returned by getContactInfo and support user actions to view directions using those addresses.
Keep the selected topic and subject in sync with URL parameters so that navigation from other pages can preconfigure the form and reloading preserves user context.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Visitor looking for contact information only
1) Visitor navigates to contact.html (optionally with topic and subject parameters).
2) On page load, the page controller calls getContactInfo with no parameters.
3) Once data is returned, the contact-info-panel shows phone_numbers, email_addresses, mailing_address, and any locations.
4) Visitor clicks a phone number to initiate a call, or an email address to open a mail client, without using the contact form.
5) If visitor needs location directions, they scroll to the location-list component and use its "view directions" interaction which constructs an external maps URL from the returned address fields.

Workflow: Visitor submitting a question (possibly from another page)
1) Visitor clicks a "Contact" link from another page (e.g., auditions.html) that includes a topic and possibly a subject in the URL (e.g., contact.html?topic=auditions&subject=Question%20about%20auditions).
2) On page load, the url-state-manager parses the topic and subject parameters and passes them as initial state into the contact-form component.
3) The contact-form initializes its topic selector and subject field with these values (if present and valid) and leaves other fields empty.
4) Visitor fills in name, email, and message, optionally adjusts the topic or subject.
5) On form submission, contact-form validates required fields on the client.
6) If valid, contact-form calls submitContactForm with the current form values (name, email, topic, subject, message).
7) When submitContactForm returns, contact-form displays the returned success status and message, and may reset or keep the form based on success.
8) The messageId returned from submitContactForm is stored in transient component state and shown to the user (for their reference) but is not written to URL parameters.

Workflow: Visitor changing topic directly on the Contact page
1) Visitor navigates to contact.html with no topic parameter.
2) Page loads and initializes contact-form with an empty or default topic.
3) Visitor selects a topic from the topic selector (e.g., auditions, scholarships, volunteering, concerts) or types their own value if the UI allows free entry.
4) The contact-form notifies url-state-manager that the topic changed.
5) url-state-manager updates the current URL (e.g., using history.replaceState) to include or modify the topic query parameter without reloading the page.
6) If the page is reloaded or the URL is shared, the same topic is restored from the URL and used to prefill the topic field again.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Browser requests contact.html.
- Behavior: page-controller component initializes, reads URL parameters (topic, subject), calls getContactInfo, and passes both the contact info data and URL-derived state into child components.
- Result: contact-info-panel and location-list render with API data once available; contact-form is initialized with the topic and subject from the URL if present.

Interaction: Click on phone number
- Trigger: User clicks the rendered phone link in contact-info-panel.
- Behavior: Browser initiates a tel: link using the number from getContactInfo.phone_numbers.
- Result: Device dialer opens with the selected number; no additional API calls are made.

Interaction: Click on email address
- Trigger: User clicks an email link in contact-info-panel.
- Behavior: Browser opens mail client via mailto: using the email from getContactInfo.email_addresses.
- Result: New email draft is created addressed to the selected contact email.

Interaction: View directions for a location
- Trigger: User clicks a "View directions" or equivalent action in location-list for a specific location.
- Behavior: Component builds an external maps URL using the address fields of that Location object returned from getContactInfo.locations.
- Result: Browser opens a new tab/window with the mapping service; no additional internal APIs are called.

Interaction: Change topic in contact form
- Trigger: User changes the topic field in contact-form (e.g., chooses a different topic from a selector).
- Behavior: contact-form updates its internal state for topic and emits an event to url-state-manager.
- Result: url-state-manager updates the topic query parameter in the current URL so that refreshing or sharing the URL preserves the new topic; no API call is made at this point.

Interaction: Change subject in contact form
- Trigger: User edits the subject field.
- Behavior: contact-form updates its internal state, and after a debounced delay, informs url-state-manager of the new subject value.
- Result: url-state-manager updates the subject query parameter (e.g., via history.replaceState) so that reloading preserves the subject text; no API call is made at this point.

Interaction: Submit contact form
- Trigger: User presses the submit button in contact-form.
- Behavior: contact-form performs client-side validation of required fields (name, email, topic, message). If validation passes, it calls submitContactForm with { name, email, topic, subject, message } from its state.
- Result: While waiting, a local "submitting" state is set to true. After submitContactForm resolves, the component uses the returned object to set success/error state, shows the returned message, optionally displays the messageId, and toggles "submitting" back to false.

Interaction: Retry submission after error
- Trigger: After a submission failure (either API-level with success=false or network-level), user clicks a "Try again" button.
- Behavior: contact-form reuses the current form state and calls submitContactForm again.
- Result: Same as initial submission flow, but the previous error state is cleared when a new attempt starts.

Interaction: Refresh page or open shared link
- Trigger: User refreshes the browser or navigates directly to a previously shared contact.html URL that includes topic and/or subject.
- Behavior: page-controller and url-state-manager parse the query parameters, initialize contact-form with those values, and call getContactInfo again.
- Result: The form is prefilled consistently across page loads and shares, and contact information is re-fetched from getContactInfo.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters as the primary external state for the contact form context.

URL parameters:
- topic (string): Optional. Used as an initial value for the contact form's topic field. Common values can include 'auditions', 'scholarships', 'volunteering', or 'concerts', but the component accepts any string value present in the URL.
- subject (string): Optional. Used as an initial value for the contact form's subject field.

Initialization logic:
1) On load, url-state-manager parses window.location.search to read topic and subject.
2) Parsed values (if any) are passed as props or initial state into contact-form.
3) contact-form uses these values as initial field content but allows the user to overwrite them.
4) Simultaneously, page-controller calls getContactInfo; while awaiting the result, contact-info-panel and location-list are in a 'loading' state.

Update logic:
1) When the user changes the topic field, contact-form updates its internal state and notifies url-state-manager.
2) url-state-manager composes a new query string from the current URL parameters, replacing or adding the topic parameter, and updates the browser URL using the History API (e.g., history.replaceState) without reloading the page.
3) When the user changes the subject field, contact-form uses a debounced update to notify url-state-manager, which then updates the subject parameter in the URL similarly.
4) If topic or subject are cleared by the user, url-state-manager removes those parameters from the query string and updates the URL accordingly.

Reload / navigation logic:
1) If the page reloads, the same initialization logic re-applies, rehydrating the contact-form's topic and subject from the URL.
2) If another page links to contact.html with topic and/or subject included, those values become the new initial state. If a parameter is missing or invalid (e.g., empty string), the corresponding field initializes to an empty value.
3) getContactInfo is always called fresh on page load; its results are not encoded into URL parameters.

Form submission state:
- The result of submitContactForm (success, messageId, message) is held only in component state and is not written to the URL. This avoids exposing transient backend identifiers in the URL while still allowing the user to see the outcome of their submission.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and shared state for the Contact page. On page load, it parses URL parameters (topic, subject), calls getContactInfo, and provides the resulting data and initial form context to child components. It also exposes a method for children to request URL updates (e.g., when topic or subject change).

Displays core contact information returned from getContactInfo, including phone numbers, email addresses, and the primary mailing address. Provides interactive links for calling and emailing, and exposes hooks for copying information to the clipboard if desired.

Lists each Location returned by getContactInfo.locations. For each location, shows its name and address fields and exposes an action to open directions in an external map service using the structured address.

Provides a general inquiry form where users enter their name, email, topic, subject, and message. Initializes its topic and subject fields from URL parameters via page-controller. On submit, validates inputs and calls submitContactForm. Displays submission status using the returned success flag, messageId, and message from the API.

Encapsulates reading and writing of URL query parameters relevant to this page (topic and subject). On initialization, it parses the current URL and provides values to page-controller. It listens to change events from contact-form and updates the URL query string (using the History API) without causing a full navigation.

Shows the current status of the most recent contact form submission (idle, submitting, success, error). It consumes the result of submitContactForm and displays the message and optionally the messageId for reference. It reacts to new submissions by resetting its state when a new submit begins.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: All contact-page components sit within a single, centered content column beneath the global header and page title/hero (handled elsewhere). Within that column, components follow a strictly top-to-bottom order: any submission feedback banner appears first (when present), followed by the general contact information panel, then the contact form, and finally the location list. Non-visual state-management components do not occupy visible space but influence these visible blocks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All contact-page components sit within a single, centered content column beneath the global header and page title/hero (handled elsewhere). Within that column, components follow a strictly top-to-bottom order: any submission feedback banner appears first (when present), followed by the general contact information panel, then the contact form, and finally the location list. Non-visual state-management components do not occupy visible space but influence these visible blocks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is visually centered on a white background, constrained to roughly 960–1100px maximum width, occupying about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop with equal left/right margins. Each major section (contact info, form, locations) is treated as its own block with internal padding of 16–24px and generous vertical separation of about 48–64px between blocks. The submission-status banner, when shown, occupies a slim but noticeable horizontal band directly above the form section, with slightly tighter vertical padding (~12–16px) to read as a message strip. Overall, whitespace around and between sections reinforces a calm, formal tone.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is low-density and editorial in feeling. Each block uses clear headings, short text groupings, and generous line spacing. There is no side-by-side crowding; instead, elements are stacked and center-aligned within the content column or left-aligned within that column, consistent with a traditional nonprofit site. The contact form fields are vertically spaced with 16–24px gaps, and labels have room to breathe, avoiding any compressed, app-like layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves down the page in a controlled vertical progression: global header and navigation at the top, then the large page title and any hero image (outside this spec), then into the contact-specific content. First, the eye catches any submission-status banner if present (color-accented but restrained), then moves to the static contact details (how to reach the organization directly), and then to the contact form as the primary interactive area. Finally, the eye naturally continues to the location list as supporting context. Within each block, left-aligned text and clear typographic hierarchy (serif headings, ample spacing) guide scanning from top to bottom.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width header and large centered 'Contact' page title (with its own generous white space and any hero image above), the main content begins as a centered column floating in a broad white background. This column, about three-quarters of the page width on desktop and capped at roughly 1000px, holds a sequence of carefully spaced sections. Any submission-status banner appears first as a subtle, full-width strip within the column, then a dignified contact-info panel lays out key email and phone details with an understated heading. Below that, separated by a wide band of whitespace, the contact form occupies the visual center of the page: a clean vertical stack of labeled fields that feels like the primary action area while still relaxed and unhurried. Further down, the location list appears as a quieter informational block, listing rehearsal and office locations without competing for attention. The entire layout reads like a formal program insert—quiet, editorial, and structured—where each section is clearly delineated by white space rather than boxes or heavy dividers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual page-state manager that does not render any visible interface. It sits conceptually at the top of the page’s content hierarchy, initializing and orchestrating the state for the contact-related components. In layout terms, it occupies no width or height and does not affect spacing; the visible layout begins immediately with the page’s title/hero (handled globally) and then the first visual contact section (submission-status banner or contact-info panel).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic-only component; participates in no visual layout and does not influence document flow or spacing directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When present, the submission-status banner appears as the first visible block within the main contact content column, directly below the page title (and any hero image) and directly above the contact-info panel. It is centered horizontally with the rest of the content, spanning the full width of the main column (roughly 70–80% of the viewport on desktop, capped around 960–1100px), with equal left and right margins to the viewport edges. The banner reads as a slim, horizontal band—about 48–64px tall depending on message length—with internal vertical padding of 12–16px and horizontal padding of around 16–24px. It has more visual weight through a soft background tint or subtle border, but retains the restrained, formal aesthetic. There is a generous gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the banner and the top of the contact-info panel, and similar spacing above it separating it from the title block, so the banner feels clearly delineated but not cramped. When hidden, the surrounding sections collapse upward so there is no empty gap left behind.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the normal document flow; appears and disappears conditionally, causing the subsequent sections (contact-info panel, form, locations) to shift up or down but otherwise maintaining the same vertical stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info panel is the first persistent content block in the contact page’s main column. It sits directly below the submission-status banner (if visible) or directly below the page title/hero area (if the banner is not visible), separated from either by roughly 48–64px of white space to maintain a calm, editorial rhythm. The panel is centered horizontally, aligned with the main content width (around 960–1100px max), with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides so the heading, short intro text, and key contact details (email, phone, mailing address) do not touch its edges. Text within the panel is mostly left-aligned within the centered column to match the traditional nonprofit feel, with the heading in a serif type sitting at the top of the panel and the information gently stacked below. There is generous vertical spacing—about 16–24px—between the heading, introductory line, and each group of contact details. The bottom of the panel leaves a wide margin (48–64px) before the top of the contact form, so each block reads as a distinct section on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky block that participates in the normal top-to-bottom document flow, maintaining consistent horizontal alignment and fixed max-width with the rest of the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form is placed immediately below the contact-info panel, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to preserve the airy, editorial structure. It shares the same centered max-width column (capped around 960–1100px) and is aligned left within that column for text and field labels. The form occupies the most vertical space of any component on the page, extending downwards as a sequence of labeled fields, text inputs, and possibly dropdowns, each separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing. At the top of the form, a clear serif heading (such as 'Send Us a Message') anchors the section, followed by a brief explanatory line, both left-aligned. Each field group maintains comfortable horizontal padding of about 16–24px from the column edges so nothing feels pressed against the sides. The submit button appears near the bottom of the form, aligned left with the input fields, with vertical spacing of 24–32px above it and 48–64px below it before the start of the location list. The overall feel is calm and formal—no multicolumn field layouts; everything is stacked in a single column to mirror the vertical flow of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block flowing in the natural document order. The form does not stick or float; it simply grows vertically as fields are added and may extend beyond the initial viewport, encouraging natural scrolling downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location-list section sits below the contact form as the final major content block. It is separated from the bottom of the form (beneath the submit button and any small helper text) by a broad band of whitespace—approximately 48–64px—to signal a shift from interactive content to supporting information. Like other sections, it is centered within the main content column, matching the same max width and left-aligned text. At the top of this section, a modest serif heading (e.g., 'Locations' or 'Where We Meet') anchors the block, followed by a short line of explanatory text if needed. Individual locations are then stacked vertically, each entry occupying the full available column width with 16–24px of vertical spacing between entries. Each location line or card might include the name (e.g., 'Rehearsal Hall'), address, and perhaps a small note on usage, all set in a calm, readable style without heavy borders. The bottom of the location list leaves an ample margin of white space—at least 64px—to ensure the page ends softly rather than abruptly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the end of the normal document flow; it does not float or fix, simply concluding the vertical sequence of content within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component synchronizes URL parameters with the page’s state (for example, preselecting a subject in the contact form or scrolling to a specific section) but does not render visible UI. It conceptually resides alongside the page-controller at the infrastructure layer, operating behind the scenes. In the visual layout, it occupies no space—no width, no height—and introduces no visible gaps between sections. Any behavior it triggers, such as auto-scrolling to the contact form or locations, respects the existing vertical layout and does not change the relative positions or sizes of the visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic-only component; does not participate in visual layout. It may influence scroll position on load, but does not alter the spatial relationships or alignment of visible sections.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: Displays the organization’s privacy policy, website terms of use, and data-handling information loaded dynamically from the server, with deep-linking to specific sections via URL parameters.

core_features: Load all policy content (privacy policy, terms of use, data-handling summary) from the getPolicies interface on page initialization.
Render privacy policy HTML content as a dedicated section that can be targeted via the URL parameter `section=privacy`.
Render terms of use HTML content as a dedicated section that can be targeted via the URL parameter `section=terms`.
Render data-handling summary content as a dedicated section that can be targeted via the URL parameter `section=data` (or equivalent configured value).
Provide an in-page section navigation (e.g., tabs or side navigation) that switches the visible policy section and updates the `section` URL parameter accordingly.
Support deep-linking from other pages (newsletter, donate, checkout, index) so that users arriving with `?section=...` land directly on the relevant policy section.
Synchronize the active section with URL state so that refreshing the page or sharing the URL preserves which policy section is visible.
Handle loading and error states for the getPolicies interface, informing the user if policies cannot be loaded and allowing retry.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Direct visit from footer link
1. User clicks the “Privacy & Terms” link in the global footer.
2. Browser navigates to `policies.html` with no `section` parameter.
3. On page load, the policies-loader component calls getPolicies.
4. While awaiting the response, a loading state is displayed in place of policy content.
5. On successful response, the page stores `privacy_policy_html`, `terms_of_use_html`, and `data_handling_summary` in page state.
6. Since no `section` is present in the URL, the default active section is selected (e.g., privacy). The navigation component highlights this section as active.
7. The corresponding policy content is rendered to the user.

Workflow: Arrival from newsletter page seeking privacy details
1. On the newsletter subscription page, the user clicks a contextual link labeled for privacy or data use.
2. Browser navigates to `policies.html?section=privacy` (or another configured value tied to the privacy section).
3. On page load, the policies-loader component calls getPolicies.
4. Once data is returned, the state-sync component interprets `section=privacy` and sets the active section to the privacy policy.
5. The navigation component highlights the privacy section.
6. The privacy policy content is scrolled into view if needed (e.g., if there is a heading element associated with privacy).
7. If the user clicks another section (e.g., terms), the active section changes and the URL is updated to `policies.html?section=terms`.

Workflow: Arrival from donation or checkout pages for data-handling info
1. On a donation or ticket checkout page, the user clicks a link about how their data is used.
2. Browser navigates to `policies.html?section=data` (or another configured value tied to data-handling information).
3. On page load, the policies-loader component calls getPolicies.
4. After successful response, the state-sync component reads `section=data` and sets the active section to the data-handling section.
5. The navigation component highlights the data-handling section.
6. The data-handling content (e.g., `data_handling_summary` along with any relevant portions of the privacy or terms HTML that include data practices) is rendered.
7. The user can switch to other sections; the URL `section` parameter is updated each time.

Workflow: Sharing a link to a specific policy section
1. A user is viewing a specific section (e.g., terms) on `policies.html` with `?section=terms` reflected in the URL.
2. The user copies the page URL from the browser address bar and shares it.
3. A recipient opens that URL; the browser loads `policies.html?section=terms`.
4. The page calls getPolicies, then sets the active section based on `section=terms`.
5. The terms of use section is immediately displayed as the primary content for the new visitor.

Workflow: Error and retry when loading policies
1. User navigates to `policies.html`.
2. The policies-loader component calls getPolicies.
3. If the request fails (e.g., network error or server error), the loader stores an error state.
4. An error message is displayed in the content area, with an option to retry loading.
5. When the user clicks the retry action, the loader calls getPolicies again.
6. On success, the error state is cleared and the policy content is shown according to the current `section` parameter.
interactions:
Click: Section navigation item (e.g., Privacy / Terms / Data Handling)
- Effect: Updates the active section in page state, updates the `section` URL parameter using history APIs (or full reload via link), and re-renders the corresponding policy content panel.
- The component ensures that only the selected section’s content is visible as the primary content area.

Page load: Initial data fetch
- Effect: On first render, the policies-loader component automatically calls getPolicies with no parameters.
- While the call is pending, a loading indicator state is displayed.
- On success, it populates the page state with the returned HTML strings and clears the loading state.
- On failure, it sets an error state visible to the user.

URL parameter read: `section`
- Effect: After getPolicies resolves, the state-sync component reads the `section` query parameter from the URL.
- If the value matches a recognized section key (e.g., `privacy`, `terms`, `data`), the active section is set accordingly.
- If the `section` parameter is missing or unrecognized, a default section (e.g., privacy) is selected.
- If the active section changes due to URL parameter, the navigation highlights update, and the corresponding content is scrolled into view.

URL update on section change
- When the user switches sections via navigation interactions, the state-sync component updates the URL query parameter `section` without reloading the page (if supported), e.g., from `policies.html?section=privacy` to `policies.html?section=terms`.
- This ensures that the back/forward browser buttons navigate between section states and that the URL accurately reflects the currently visible section.

Click: Retry loading policies
- When an error state is visible, the user can click a retry action.
- Effect: Triggers a new call to getPolicies.
- The loading indicator is displayed again until the request resolves, and then either the content or another error is shown.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the URL query parameter `section`. Valid values are mapped to internal section identifiers (e.g., 'privacy', 'terms', 'data'). On page initialization, the policies-loader component calls getPolicies. Once the data is loaded, the state-sync logic reads the current URL `section` parameter and sets the active section accordingly. If `section` is absent or does not match any supported section, the page defaults to a predetermined section (e.g., privacy). When the user switches sections via the navigation component, the page updates its internal active section and uses the browser history API or standard anchor navigation to update `section` in the URL without relying on localStorage. This approach ensures that reloading the page or navigating with back/forward buttons maintains the correct section. All other state (loading, error, active section) is held in ephemeral in-memory page state and is not persisted outside the current page context.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches all policy content using the getPolicies interface, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting privacy policy HTML, terms of use HTML, and data-handling summary to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getPolicies() with no parameters, set pageState.loading=true before the call, then on success set pageState.loading=false and store returned values in pageState.privacyPolicyHtml, pageState.termsOfUseHtml, and pageState.dataHandlingSummary. On failure, set pageState.loading=false and pageState.error to an error descriptor.
onRetryClick: When triggered by a user retry action, repeat the getPolicies() call, resetting the loading and error states as needed.

components.functionality: Synchronizes the active policy section with the URL `section` parameter. Interprets URL state after policy content has loaded and ensures that navigation interactions update the URL accordingly without page reload where possible.
event_handlers: onPoliciesLoaded: After policies-loader successfully populates page state, read URL.query.section and map it to pageState.activeSection. If URL.query.section matches a known key (e.g., 'privacy', 'terms', 'data'), set pageState.activeSection to that value; otherwise, set a default section (e.g., 'privacy').
onUrlChange: If the URL `section` parameter changes (e.g., via browser back/forward navigation), update pageState.activeSection to match the new value and trigger a re-render of the visible section.

onActiveSectionChange: When pageState.activeSection is updated from any source (navigation click or URL change), update the URL `section` parameter using the browser history API (if available), ensuring the current URL always reflects the active section. Optionally scroll the associated section container into view.

components.functionality: Provides user controls to switch between policy sections (such as privacy policy, terms of use, and data-handling information). Highlights the currently active section and triggers changes to the active section when a navigation item is clicked.
event_handlers: onClickPrivacy: Set pageState.activeSection to the internal key mapped to the privacy policy section (e.g., 'privacy'). This triggers policy-state-sync to update the URL `section` parameter accordingly.
onClickTerms: Set pageState.activeSection to the internal key mapped to the terms of use section (e.g., 'terms'). This triggers policy-state-sync to update the URL `section` parameter accordingly.

onClickDataHandling: Set pageState.activeSection to the internal key mapped to the data-handling section (e.g., 'data'). This triggers policy-state-sync to update the URL `section` parameter accordingly.

components.functionality: Renders the appropriate policy content section based on the current active section. Uses the HTML strings returned from getPolicies and may render them as separate panels or conditionally visible sections.
event_handlers: onRender: If pageState.loading is true, render a loading state instead of policy content. If pageState.error is set, render an error message and expose a retry action that triggers policies-loader.onRetryClick.

onSectionChange: When pageState.activeSection changes, re-render to show only the corresponding section’s content. For the privacy section, inject pageState.privacyPolicyHtml into the DOM; for terms, inject pageState.termsOfUseHtml; for data-handling, render pageState.dataHandlingSummary and, optionally, any relevant parts of the other HTML if the layout supports it.

components.functionality: Provides a user-triggered control to retry loading policy content if the initial getPolicies call fails.
event_handlers: onClick: When visible (only if pageState.error is set), invoke policies-loader.onRetryClick to re-attempt fetching content from getPolicies.

layout.grid_system: Implicit centered content column likely based on a 12-column desktop grid with a max-width content container (~960–1140px) and full-width header. Content is visually centered rather than edge-to-edge.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing full-width site header, the Policies page uses a single, centered content column. The sequence from top to bottom is: (1) a generous page-title band with the word “Policies” (and optional short description) centered within the main column; (2) a horizontal policy section navigation (tabs) sitting just below the title, aligned with the same centered column; (3) the policy content display occupying the bulk of the vertical space beneath the tabs; (4) conditional system UI such as loading state and error/retry appearing within that same column where the content would normally appear. All components stack vertically in a calm, editorial flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing full-width site header, the Policies page uses a single, centered content column. The sequence from top to bottom is: (1) a generous page-title band with the word “Policies” (and optional short description) centered within the main column; (2) a horizontal policy section navigation (tabs) sitting just below the title, aligned with the same centered column; (3) the policy content display occupying the bulk of the vertical space beneath the tabs; (4) conditional system UI such as loading state and error/retry appearing within that same column where the content would normally appear. All components stack vertically in a calm, editorial flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column is constrained to approximately 960–1140px max width and centered on the page with wide white margins on both left and right. The page title band occupies a substantial vertical slice with 64px–96px total vertical padding, clearly separated from the header and subsequent content. The navigation tabs occupy a relatively shallow band but have comfortable 16px–24px spacing above and below. The main policy content display is given the most vertical space, extending well below the fold with ample line spacing and 24px–32px separation between subsections. Loading and error states appear within the same column, with generous whitespace surrounding them so they feel integrated rather than cramped.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and calm. Each major block (title, tabs, content, system messages) is separated by 24px–48px of vertical whitespace. Text lines are kept at a readable width (around 60–75 characters), with line height slightly relaxed and paragraph spacing in the 16px–24px range. Navigation tabs are evenly spaced and not tightly packed, mirroring the understated nonprofit aesthetic. There is no heavy use of borders or boxes; instead, whitespace and typography create structure.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the global header down to the large, centered “Policies” title, which acts as the main visual anchor. From there, the gaze drops slightly to the policy section navigation tabs arranged in a single horizontal line. The user then moves downward into the left-aligned body copy of the policy content display, scrolling vertically through sections. Within the tabs, there is a mild left-to-right scan; within the content area, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom, with clear headings acting as waypoints. Loading and error states briefly interrupt the content area but remain contained within the same vertical flow, so the overall reading experience remains consistent and dignified.

layout.overall_description: The Policies page sits beneath the full-width site header and presents itself as a quiet, editorial column on a white background. At the top of this column, centered horizontally on the page, a large serif page title “Policies” appears with generous white space above and below, giving it the feeling of a cover heading. Just below this, a single horizontal row of policy section tabs—such as Privacy Policy, Terms of Use, and Data Handling—stretches across the central column, aligned to the same invisible grid and spaced evenly to preserve a sense of calm. Beneath the tabs, the main policy text flows downward in a left-aligned, book-like format: moderate line length, comfortable line spacing, and clearly separated sections that can be deep-linked. When content is loading or an error occurs, a simple, centered message and a retry button appear in the same content area without breaking the overall alignment. The result is a vertical, scrollable page with a strong central column and abundant white space, maintaining the traditional, understated aesthetic of a classical arts nonprofit.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the same centered content column as the policy text. When active, it appears directly below the page title band and the policy section navigation area, replacing the policy-content-display region. Visually, this presents as a compact block centered horizontally within the column, approximately 40–60% of the column width, with a subtle loading indicator and short status text. There is at least 32px–48px of whitespace above and below this loader block, so it feels comfortably isolated within the white page background. The loader does not extend to the full viewport height but sits in the upper half of the visible content area, allowing the header and title to remain visible above it, preserving the vertical hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content flow; it appears and disappears dynamically in the space where the policy-content-display would normally render, without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy state sync component is non-visual and does not manifest as a visible element in the layout. Conceptually, it is anchored to the Policies page’s main content container, listening to URL parameters and scroll positions. It occupies no physical height or width and introduces no gaps; instead, it silently controls which tab is highlighted and which section of the policy content is revealed or scrolled into view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered helper behavior; it exists in the DOM but has no spatial or visual footprint and does not affect the layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy section navigation appears immediately below the page title band within the centered content column. It spans roughly 70–80% of the column width, centered horizontally, with the tab labels aligned left within that span. Each tab appears as a text label (with subtle underline or bottom-border for the active tab) arranged in a single horizontal row. Tabs have equal spacing between them (16px–24px), ensuring no sense of crowding. There is a comfortable 24px–32px margin above the tabs separating them from the bottom of the title band, and another 24px–32px margin below them before the policy-content-display begins. The overall height of the tab bar is modest—roughly the height of a single line of text plus 8px–12px vertical padding—so it feels like a refined navigation accent rather than a heavy UI control bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable content; it scrolls away with the page rather than sticking to the top, maintaining the simple editorial reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy content display is the primary body area and sits directly below the policy-section-navigation within the same centered column. It uses the full width of the content column for headings but effectively presents body text at a comfortable reading width (approximately 60–75 characters per line), which in practice feels like about 60–70% of the column width on large screens. All text is left-aligned with generous line height and 16px–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs. Section headings (e.g., H2 or H3) are spaced with 32px–48px above and 16px–24px below, acting as clear landmarks for deep-linked sections. The block stretches vertically, occupying the majority of the page’s scrollable height and framed on both sides by wide white margins, reinforcing the calm, editorial feel. Any internal lists or sub-sections keep to the same restrained typographic system, without heavy boxes or colored panels, so the layout remains clean and dignified.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content within the central column; it expands in height based on the amount of policy text and moves naturally with page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs, the error retry component appears in the same central content column where the policy-content-display would be. A short, concise error message is centered horizontally within the column with ample white space (32px–48px) above and below. Directly beneath this message, the retry button is centered and sized modestly—approximately the natural width of its label text plus 12px–16px horizontal padding on each side and 8px–10px vertical padding. There is a small gap of about 16px between the error message and the button. The entire error block occupies roughly 30–40% of the column width and sits in the upper to middle portion of the viewport, beneath the title and tabs, maintaining the same vertical rhythm. The button style is understated but clearly clickable, in keeping with the nonprofit’s restrained aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content flow; it appears only in error states, replacing or overlaying the policy-content-display area without any fixed or sticky positioning.
